title: "Chadwick saw that the lyricism of hip-hop was powerfully dramatic and theatrical"
author: thecrushbar
raw_article:
Hello, and welcome to The Crush Bar, a newsletter about theatre by Fergus Morgan.

This is the free Friday issue, which usually contains an interview with a theatremaker or an essay on a theatre-related topic. This week, it is a chat with Tristan Fynn-Aiduenu, whose production of Chadwick Boseman’s Deep Azure opens at the Sam Wanamaker Playhouse soon. After that, there are your usual three show recs.

In case you missed it, here is this week’s issue of Shouts and Murmurs, which is a weekly round-up of the most interesting writing about theatre elsewhere…

Usually, you can get Shouts and Murmurs straight in your inbox every Tuesday - and help keep this newsletter going - by signing up as a paid supporter of The Crush Bar.

Theatres, companies, agencies and other organisations can also sign-up as champion supporters of The Crush Bar, which gets their name in this dedicated tab on The Crush Bar’s Substack homepage and in the footer of every Friday issue.

Subscribe now

There is probably not much point in doing that now, though, as this will be the last issue of The Crush Bar for quite a while, as I am off travelling for twelve weeks on Monday. That issue above explains everything. I will be pausing payments straight after pressing send on this. Any questions about that, feel free to get in touch.

There is something deeply poignant about the late Chadwick Boseman’s Deep Azure being staged at Shakespeare’s Globe.

Boseman, who tragically died aged 43 in August 2020, is known to most as Marvel superhero Black Panther. Prior to international stardom, though, he was heavily involved in hip-hop theatre. Motivated by a theatre course he attended at the University of Oxford in 1998 during a summer break from Washington DC’s Howard University, Boseman wanted, in the words of collaborator Derek Sanders, to “take urban prose and poetry and kind of lift it up like Shakespearean verse.” In 2005, he wrote a play, Deep Azure. Inspired by the killing of fellow Howard student Prince Jones at the hands of the police in September 2000, it tells the story of a young woman, Azure, struggling with an eating disorder after the death of her fiancé, Deep, and, echoing Shakespeare, it uses the rhythms and rhymes of hip-hop to do so. It received short runs in New York and Chicago in 2005, but has not been performed since, despite Boseman professing a desire to return to it. Now, although he is no longer alive to see it, Deep Azure is being staged in the Sam Wanamaker Playhouse.

That is happening thanks to director Tristan Fynn-Aiduenu. Born and raised in South London, where he still lives, Fynn-Aiduenu first made a name for himself in 2019 when he won the JMK Award and staged a five-star revival of Arinzé Kene’s Little Baby Jesus at Richmond’s Orange Tree Theatre. The production that really put him on the map, though, was For Black Boys Who Have Considered Suicide When The Hue Gets Too Heavy, Ryan Calais Cameron’s acclaimed examination of mental health among Black men, which opened at the New Diorama Theatre in 2022, then ran at the Royal Court, then transferred into the West End. Now, Fynn-Aiduenu - an articulate and infectiously exuberant interviewee, which probably explains why I have spoken to him twice before - is directing Deep Azure. We spoke about Boseman’s play, his relationship with it, its journey to the stage, and more via Zoom earlier this week.

Tristan, this is the third time I’ve interviewed you. That is a record for me. Is it a record for you?

I don’t think I’ve been interviewed by the same person three times. You’re like that interviewer that always came back to Muhammad Ali. I’m Muhammad Ali.

Sure. Tell me about Deep Azure. How did you discover the play?

I actually found the play in early 2020, while I was looking for things to do. The choreographer Jade Hackett had said to me: ‘Tristan, I think you would do well in hip-hop theatre.’ She said what I was doing was on the borders of what is called hip-hop theatre. I said: ‘Cool.’ I looked for books about hip-hop theatre, and found this anthology called Say Word!, and I read it. Deep Azure was the first play I read. I thought it was beautiful but I did not feel ready to direct it. I just didn’t get it yet.

You didn’t realise that it was by Chadwick Boseman at first, though, right?

Right. It was when I read it for a second time that I realised it was by Chadwick Boseman. I was like: ‘Oh my days, the Chadwick Boseman.’ That gave me my second reason to do it. I wanted to do it so Chadwick could see it. Sometimes, as a writer, you can feel like your work isn’t mainstream enough and will get forgotten. I wanted to him to see that his play hadn’t been forgotten. Then he died. At first, I didn’t want to do it anymore, because what was the point? Then, I thought, what if I did it to honour him, and to let everyone else know about his work?

You still didn’t feel ready, though. What happened to change that?

I knew I had a real affinity with the play, but I didn’t fully understand it yet. Then my own father passed. When that happened, I reread the play and realised how much grief runs through it. It was only in going in understanding my own grief and exploring it and riding it that I felt like I really understood the play. I was like: ‘Now I get this. Now I can be in a room and talk about it.’

Tell me about the play itself then. What is it about?

Chadwick wrote this in grief for someone he knew, Prince Jones, a student who was killed by police in 2000. The play is not totally true. It is about Chadwick, though, and it is about Prince. It is about this student with all these ideas that was on his way to being a brilliant man, who had his time cut short. I think Chadwick would be pleased that we are doing this in honour of him, but I think he would be far more pleased that we are doing it in honour of Prince.

What does grief mean in this play? What does it mean to you?

There is that line from Marvel’s WandaVision: ‘What is grief, if not love persevering?’ I will always love that line because it is true. Grief is still loving someone that is no longer on this earth anymore. Their spirit is, though. Their essence lives within me. And you have to learn to move with that. That is grief.

A Black man being killed by police has obvious resonances. Is this a political play?

It is political. Everything is political. It is definitely a real dissection and comment on the way systems can kill Black people. Significantly, though, the police officer that killed Prince was also Black. And the police officer that kills Deep in the play is also Black. That is a huge political statement. The play shows how racism is a many tentacled monster. It finds its way into a variety of things, including tribalism, including colourism, including body image, and particularly how women view their own bodies, and how hip-hop and white media has shaped what it means to be seen as desirable. All these things are very political. How Black people grieve is a very political thing, too. How Black people love each other is a political thing. It is a revolutionary thing because we live in a system that sees us as a risk and a threat. We talk a lot about Black trauma and how tired we are of seeing it on stages and on screens and in books. This play does talk about a traumatic incident, but not for another gaze, not as porn for someone else. This play explores it in a different way, and that is a political thing to do.

Tell me about Boseman’s ideas about Shakespeare and hip-hop.

From what I know, Chadwick did a British American Drama Academy course called Midsummer In Oxford, which was paid for by Phylicia Rashad and Denzel Washington, among others. If he already had a love of Shakespeare, it effervesced during that period. He saw, though, that hip-hop was the same as Shakespearean verse. In Shakespeare, poetry has a dramatic function. Chadwick saw that the lyricism of hip-hop and the culture of hip-hop was powerfully dramatic and theatrical, too, and could be put on stage. That is why I wanted to do it somewhere that really upholds Shakespeare. I wanted to put it on the same stage as an equal.

That is why it is significant that this is being staged at Shakespeare’s Globe, I guess. It is in the candlelit Sam Wanamaker Playhouse. What will your staging be like?

All I will say is that it is a spectacle of words and voice and movement and of Blackness. That is me. That is my MO.

Do you feel like you are in exactly the right place, directing exactly the right show?

I really do. It feels like all of my ideas about theatre are melding together and that really excites me. I am excited about the conversations around it, too. One of my goals as a director is to bring Black people together to connect. We don’t need to agree, but I do think we need to connect.

Will there be a Black Out night during the run? It seems like there should be?

We have had conversations about that. I will say that it is something that I would really like because I have been in Black Out nights as an audience member and there is nothing like it. There is nothing like sitting in a space as a Black person and not worrying about how you are watching something. You can laugh, you can scream, you can cry, you can connect. I would love to do that with this. It is not the only thing we are doing, though. We are doing a lot to make sure that the audience is not the standard, white, middle-class audience. We are doing a lot to make sure that the idea of Shakespeare doesn’t put people off. We are doing a lot to make sure that this play speaks to a wider demographic that might not usually be here. Jade Franks and Sel Adonu are helping with that. They have got loads of ideas, from workshops to ticket deals to breakout rooms. We are going to execute some.

Tell me your origin story as a theatremaker again.

I did a summer school at Ovalhouse, which is now Brixton House, and that is where I fell in love with theatre. I ended up doing an apprenticeship there with Toby Clark, where I learned how to be an assistant director. That is where I had my very first play put on as part of a festival. From there, I studied theatre at college and at university. At the same time, I did a training course for young directors with StoneCrabs Theatre. That is where I did a scratch version of one of my favourite plays in the world, Arinze Kene’s Little Baby Jesus. Then I did a few different jobs, but the one that really helped was being assistant director on The Brothers Size at the Young Vic under Bijan Sheibani. That was a brilliant experience. It gave me the confidence to apply for the JMK Award. One of the five plays you could pick in the application was Little Baby Jesus, so obviously I picked that, and did everything I could to get it. I did get it. I cried. And I got to direct Little Baby Jesus at the Orange Tree Theatre. That is how I connected with Ryan Calais Cameron and ended up doing For Black Boys at the New Diorama.

For Black Boys had such an incredible journey. Could you feel that energy in it?

I could feel that, but what people have to remember is that before For Black Boys, there was Jessica Hagan’s Queens Of Sheba. Ryan had already built a name for himself with that, and that audience came to see For Black Boys. It was a time when people were talking about men’s mental health a lot, too. Everything aligned and it snowballed. A young producer at the Royal Court, Jasmyn Fisher-Ryner, came to see it and really fought for it. It went to the Royal Court. It went to the West End. It was amazing. And it has opened a lot of doors for me, so I am very thankful.

What is next for you?

Next, I am doing Death Of A Salesman at the Liverpool Everyman. I’m very excited about that. Then there are so many things I want to do. I have got a crazy Othello in mind. I am writing again, too. I am writing this epic about Ghanaian demon slayers in the UK. I have no idea how it is going to work. I have set up a company called The Black Phantastic focused on doing Black fantasy, Black spirituality and Black sci-fi on stage because I don’t think we see a lot of that. We haven’t done a production yet, but we have done some wonderful workshops.

You seem to have so much energy and enthusiasm, Tristan.

I’ve just never stopped. I’ve never stopped thinking. I’ve never stopped creating. I’ve never stopped pitching. I’ve never stopped because this is what I love to do. That would be my advice to younger directors. Don’t stop but get smart. Let’s be real. It isn’t just about working really hard. It is also about working smart. It is about knowing exactly what you are doing, like I did when I pitched this play. I was very specific. It is about getting specific and working hard at that.

Deep Azure is at the Sam Wanamaker Playhouse until April 11.

Help keep The Crush Bar going by signing up as a paid supporter for £5/month or £50/year, or a champion supporter for £250/year.

Three shows to see next week

Ballad Lines - Southwark Playhouse, until March 21

I saw an earlier iteration of Finn Anderson and Tania Azevedo’s musical in Stirling three years ago and loved it. It is an ambitious, expansive, evocative show about migration, motherhood, music and more, and I am thrilled that it is getting a longer run in London, and keeping my fingers crossed for a return to Scotland. You can read last week’s interview with Anderson here and get tickets via the button below.

Book tickets here

All Is But Fantasy - The Other Place, until February 21

It might be struggling financially, but the Royal Shakespeare Company is excelling artistically under Daniel Evans and Tamara Harvey. Next, Brooklyn-based, Tony Award-nominated actor, musician and director Whitney White is arriving in Stratford with a double-bill of gig-theatre shows that see the characters of Lady Macbeth, Emilia, Juliet, and Richard III step up to the microphone. You can read Arifa Akbar’s interview with White here in The Guardian, and get tickets via the button below.

Book tickets here

The Virgins - Soho Theatre, until March 7

Miriam Battye, a writer on HBO’s Succession, seems intent on staging the honest conversations we never have. Her two-handed comedy Strategic Love Play, a hit at the 2023 Edinburgh Festival Fringe, was a winningly frank portrait of modern dating. Now, Battye is back with The Virgins, which focuses on four girls and two boys alone in a house, who decide to stop pretending. Directed by Jaz Woodcock-Stewart, it runs at Soho Theatre until early March. You can get tickets via the button below.

Book tickets here

title: "There is a reason certain songs survive. There is a reason that people keep singing them."
author: thecrushbar
raw_article:
Hello, and welcome to The Crush Bar, a newsletter about theatre by Fergus Morgan.

This is the free Friday issue, which usually contains an interview with a theatremaker or an essay on a theatre-related topic. This week, it is a chat with writer and composer Finn Anderson, whose show Ballad Lines starts performances at Southwark Playhouse next week. After that, there are your usual three show recommendations.

In case you missed it, here is this week’s issue of Shouts and Murmurs, which is a weekly round-up of the most interesting writing about theatre elsewhere…

You can get Shouts and Murmurs straight in your inbox every Tuesday - and help keep this newsletter going - by signing up as a paid supporter of The Crush Bar.

Theatres, companies, agencies and other organisations can also sign-up as champion supporters of The Crush Bar, which gets their name in this dedicated tab on The Crush Bar’s Substack homepage and in the footer of every Friday issue.

Get the discount here

Three years ago, at Stirling’s Macrobert Arts Centre, I fell in love with a new musical.

Created by director Tania Azavedo and musician Finn Anderson, it tells the story of several generations of women in the same family, stretching from sixteenth-century Scotland, through seventeenth-century Ulster, to contemporary America, with the same themes of unexpected pregnancy and impossible choices recurring along the way. What makes the show so special, though, is the music. Traditional folk songs, which started life in Scotland, were carried across the Atlantic by migrants, and are still sung in the Appalachian Mountains of West Virginia today, have been reworked and threaded through layered, infectious original numbers. The result, as I wrote in my five-star review for The Stage three years ago, is “an ambitious, expansive, evocative show about music and motherhood that brims with heart and history.” Back in 2023, the show was called A Mother’s Song. Now, it has been renamed Ballad Lines, and it is finally returning for an eight-week run at Southwark Playhouse.

It is not the first musical from Anderson that has struck a chord. The writer, composer and singer-songwriter was born and raised in Fife, studied at Perth College and Goldsmiths, and started creating shows in London in the early 2010s. It was in 2018, though, after Anderson had returned to Scotland, that his two-handed, live-looped, folklore-infused musical Islander first appeared. Co-created with Stewart Melton, it was a big hit at the Edinburgh Fringe in 2019, ran at Southwark Playhouse later that year, and has since been staged around the world, adapted into a BBC Radio 4 drama and made into a film. I chatted to Anderson about all that via Zoom last week.

A Mother’s Song is finally back, with a new name, Ballad Lines. You must be thrilled.

I am. It is always great to revisit a show with a few more years of experience, with fresh voices and fresh takes and a new audience. We always felt really strongly about that five-day run in Stirling not being the end of the road. It has taken a little while to figure out what the next steps should be, and the material has come a long way since then, but I am really delighted that we get to share it with more people.

What has changed from the version I saw in Stirling three years ago?

We’ve done a lot of work on the script, fleshing out the characters and their relationships and the choices they make. It feels like their stories are so much clearer and richer now. There are four new songs, too, and three of the songs that were there in 2023 have been cut. The pacing is a bit tighter. The musical arrangements have shifted. We made an album and really worked on them in the studio. We brought a banjo player on board, Kaia Kater, who is amazing. Anna Massie is a new addition on the guitar, and so is Charlotte Printer on the bass. They have helped us get the most out of the songs. The staging is pretty much all new, too. There is a lot more movement and choreography. We’ve got a new design. Tania has grown a lot as a director since 2023. This version is in thrust, so it feels more intimate and electric. The heart of the show has remained, though.

Where did the journey start with this show?

Tania, the show’s co-creator, co-writer and director, and I met in 2014 at the Edinburgh Fringe over coffee and bonded over a lot of shared interests and passions. In 2017, we were commissioned by the Royal Conservatoire of Scotland and the American Musical Theatre Project, which is based at Northwestern University in Chicago, to write a musical for a transatlantic group of students at the Fringe, so it had to be 55 minutes in length, but for a big cast. Andrew Panton, who is now at Dundee Rep, was at RCS at the time. He initiated it, then I got Tania involved. At the time, I was really interested in the migration of songs, and specifically in the migration of Scottish ballads and Scottish folk music in general. Because it was a transatlantic commission, it felt like a good opportunity to explore that. Tania came with a lot of questions around motherhood and queer parenthood and female choice over the generations. We crafted a story and characters that wove together those two threads. It became clear for us during that process that this was something we wanted to develop beyond a 55-minute Fringe musical.

How did you develop it from there?

It was performed at the Fringe in 2018. After that, we were invited for a five-week residency in Chicago at the beginning of 2020, when we fleshed it out from a one-act piece into a two-act piece, expanded the plot, wrote a lot of new songs and went deeper into the things we were interested exploring. We had to fly home early because of Covid, though. A year after that, we did a reading of it on the main stage at The Lowry during lockdown to an audience of fifty. From there, we started talking with the Scotland-based producer Kate Taylor, who came on board. Then we got Creative Scotland funding to do the first professional production in 2023.

How did you find the traditional songs in the show? And how did you work them in?

I went on a hunt for ballads, and specifically for ballads that are alive in Appalachia today that would have originated in Scotland. Of course, all of this stuff is really fluid. Songs don’t really have a beginning and an end point, especially folk songs. There are songs sung in Scotland and Appalachia that are also sung in France, you know? Anyway, I did a lot of reading, and online research, and talking to singers. The story was evolving simultaneously. Images and characters from the ballads informed the story. Then the story sent me looking for ballads that spoke to it. Then I found more ballads that took the story in different directions. I’ve never written a jukebox musical, and never had the desire to, to be honest, but in a way there was an element of that to the process. So many jukebox musicals don’t work because there is no drama, no stakes, no turning points in the songs, though. These songs are full of that stuff, so it is easier to craft them into a narrative.

Can you give me an example of that process with a specific song?

The song that immediately springs to mind is The Four Marys. There are lots of different versions of that song with different melodies and different lyrics, but it basically tells the story of a lady-in-waiting who has a child with a king. In some versions, she kills her baby. In others, she was forced to have the baby then killed herself. It’s a very dark story. In the musical, though, three different women across three timelines have different relationships to it. One sings it to comfort herself. One sings it to scare her sister. It was about finding different concepts like that. The fun thing is playing around with how songs like that might sound in historical Scotland and how they might sound in the world of Appalachian bluegrass.

It must be a joy to work with such rich material.

It is. There is a reason certain songs survive. There is a reason that people keep singing them. It is because the stories they contain come around and around again. It’s amazing to take a song that is 600 years old, put it in a contemporary context, and for it to resonate just as much as it would have done back then. It is a total joy to work with material that is so rich like that. This show could be five hours long if we let ourselves run away with it because there is so much amazing stuff.

How did you go about orchestrating those traditional songs?

Whereas crafting the story is quite an intellectual process, crafting the music is a very organic, instinctive process for me. A lot of the ballads are originally a capella, so you can do whatever you want underneath them to give them a different emotional layer. I played with that a lot, keeping the melody the same but shifting what is underneath it harmonically to make it lighter or darker, or move it to a different location, or shifting the melody to a minor key here and there. Then it was a matter of placing verses in different scenes so they worked for the story, and weaving them around the original songs in the show. There are eighteen original songs in the show, four songs that are entirely traditional, and the rest are a hybrid.

The music is Scottish. You’re Scottish. Why is this not premiering in Scotland?

We were just lucky to find a partner down here with Katy Lipson and Aria Entertainment. She has been aware of the show for a few years, but it kind of landed on her desk again at a good moment for her as a new mother, and she just fell in love with it. It felt like the right fit. She was also able to get the resource together to make it happen in the way we wanted. I would love to bring it back to Scotland. I would love for it to tour Scotland. I would love this version to come home. It is a matter of resource and will, though, and this is where we found that, I guess. I can’t say with any certainty that it will come back to Scotland, but I will do everything in my power. It would be nice for me, given that I live there.

Where did you grow up?

I grew up in the East Neuk of Fife, and I’ve just moved back there, actually. Growing up, my parents took me to shows at Dundee Rep and the Byre Theatre in St Andrews. I just loved it. I would make up plays at home. I saw musicals, too, and that is what inspired me to start writing songs on the little keyboard I had. All through primary school, I was making musicals at lunchtime with friends. That’s how I learned music. I’m not classically trained. I studied music at school then studied popular music at Perth College, then moved to London and did a course in musical theatre writing at Goldsmiths, but my relationship with it has always been about writing and creating and improvising than learning existing pieces. I’m not technically the best musician, but I think my skill is in storytelling through song.

What did you do after graduating from Goldsmiths?

I stuck around in London for a couple of years. I did a lot of teaching. I wrote a musical called Streets that had a lovely production there and opened the door to different connections. That gave me a real boost. I moved to Glasgow in 2014 and did a show at the Fringe with some friends. It was called Alba, which I’m not proud of. It was long before Alex Salmond’s party, though. That was the year I met Tania.

The first work of yours I saw was Islander, which has had a remarkable journey, from Edinburgh Fringe show to BBC Radio 4 drama to productions around the world.

It has, and it is still having this amazing life. There’s a production about to open in Mexico. There were productions in Poland and France last year. There have been productions all over the world. It is so cool. As you can probably tell, I’m passionate about songs being reinvented, and I really love the idea that there are people out there doing their own thing with Islander.

You also work as a singer-songwriter. How do you divide your time?

It is probably 80 per-cent theatre and 20 per-cent my own stuff, and I’m quite happy with it that way. I’ve always wanted to do both, and I will continue doing both as long as I can. I love being in a room full of collaborators in a really intensive process, but the best thing at the end of that is to lock myself in a room and write some songs that no-one else has a say in. Musical theatre is a constant compromise because the overall show is the most important thing. In musicals, a song has to have a turning point and be lived in by a character. Writing a song outside that is different. It can just exist as a feeling for people to sit in.

How hard is it to develop new musicals in Scotland?

Scotland is home and I’m very determined to continue living and working there. That is not a question for me. I would love to be developing a bunch of musicals all at once in Scotland, but, for whatever reason, that is just not that possible. I have also been lucky in that other opportunities have presented themselves elsewhere. I am an associate at The Lowry in Salford at the moment, for example. It is not that working in Scotland is shit at all. I love it, and I have great relationships with a lot of venues and producers in Scotland. I could keep making Islanders in Scotland. There is just a limited amount of opportunity for developing musicals at this scale.

It seems like that situation is changing, though, with people like Andrew Panton at Dundee Rep driving development, independent producers like Raw Material Arts taking risks, and Alan Cumming bringing in big artists from elsewhere.

I think that cross-border collaboration and international collaboration is a good thing. Scotland is a small country. It is great when we can learn from people elsewhere, and when people elsewhere learn from us because we have a lot to give. When it comes to musicals, though, the reality is that we just don’t have the same history and experience and understanding as people do elsewhere in the world, particularly, boringly, people in America and London. We do, though, have an incredibly rich tradition of music and storytelling, so I think we all have a lot to learn from each other. I don’t think that means Scottish theatre has to lose its identity, and I think it is important that people in Scotland are employed and given opportunities, too, but those things should not be mutually exclusive.

Ballad Lines is at Southwark Playhouse until March 21.

Three shows to see next week

A Grain Of Sand - Arcola Theatre, until January 31

First seen in Liverpool last year, this one-woman show blends Palestinian folklore and real-life testimonies from people living in contemporary Gaza to explore war through the eyes of a child. Adapted and directed by Elias Matar from Leila Boukarim and Asaf Luzon’s collection A Million Kites, it is produced by Good Chance, the remarkable company behind The Jungle and Kyoto, and runs at the Arcola Theatre for ten days, before touring to Leeds, Mold, Canterbury, Bristol, Edinburgh, Glasgow, Plymouth, Oxford, Coventry, and Manchester. You can get tickets via the button below.

Book tickets here

Gerry and Sewell - Aldwych Theatre, until January 24

Adapted from Jonathan Tulloch’s 2000 novel The Season Ticket, which was made into the film Purely Belter the same year, this comedy musical from writer and director Jamie Eastlake focuses on two broke Geordie lads desperately trying to get their hands on Newcastle United season tickets. First seen at Laurels, Whitley Bay, in 2022, it is now running in the West End for a fortnight, ahead of a home leg back on Tyneside in June. It is “hilarious from start to finish” according to The Independent’s Alice Saville, who gave it four stars. You can get tickets via the button below.

Book tickets here

The Tempest - Shakespeare’s Globe, until April 12

Experimental theatre legend Tim Crouch’s first show for The Globe is a strange spin on The Tempest, which reimagines Prospero, Miranda, Ariel and Caliban as stranded souls with no hope of escape, telling each other Shakespeare’s story as a ritual on their isolated island. Crouch himself stars as Prospero, with Sophie Steer as Miranda, Naomi Wirthner as Ariel and Faizal Abdullah as Caliban. It runs in the candlelit Sam Wanamaker Playhouse until mid-April. You can get tickets via the button below.

title: "I was surprised by the classism at Cambridge. I didn’t expect people to say awful things about Liverpool to my face."
author: thecrushbar
raw_article:
Hello, and welcome to The Crush Bar, a newsletter about theatre by Fergus Morgan.

This is the free Friday issue, which usually contains an interview with a theatremaker or an essay on a theatre-related topic. This week, it is a chat with Jade Franks, whose Edinburgh Fringe hit Eat The Rich (but maybe not me mates x) starts a three-week run at Soho Theatre next week. After that, there are your usual three show recommendations.

In case you missed it, here is this week’s issue of Shouts and Murmurs, which is a weekly round-up of the most interesting writing about theatre elsewhere…

You can get Shouts and Murmurs straight in your inbox every Tuesday - and help keep this newsletter going - by signing up as a paid supporter of The Crush Bar.

Theatres, companies, agencies and other organisations can also sign-up as champion supporters of The Crush Bar, which gets their name in this dedicated tab on The Crush Bar’s Substack homepage and in the footer of every Friday issue.

Get the discount here

Every few years, someone explodes at the Edinburgh Fringe. Last summer, it was Jade Franks.

Franks’ debut solo show Eat The Rich (but maybe not me mates x) focuses on her experiences as a working-class student at Cambridge University. With an exuberant mix of brashness and bemusement, Franks relates how she arrived at Cambridge and was confronted and confounded by arcane rituals, social hierarchies and extraordinary privilege. Directed by Tatenda Shamiso and produced by Jasmyn Fisher-Ryner, the show was a total sell-out in Edinburgh, earning a slew of glowing reviews, including a festival-changing five-star write-up from The Guardian’s Mark Fisher, plus a Fringe First Award and comparisons to everything from Brideshead Revisited to Fleabag, although those are not nods with which Franks feels entirely comfortable.

Franks, who hails from Wallasey on the Wirral, has a Liverpool season ticket, and speaks in a glorious scouse accent, also works in outreach. Alongside her snowballing career as a writer and actor, she helps theatres and productions - Ryan Calais Cameron’s For Black Boys, the current West End revival of All My Sons, the forthcoming production of Deep Azure at Shakespeare’s Globe - build diverse audiences. We chatted about her plans on that front, about Mo Salah, and about the extraordinary success of Eat The Rich ahead of the show’s three-week stint at Soho Theatre, after which it will visit Liverpool and Bristol. Oh, and a Netflix adaptation, produced by Adolescence’s Phil Barantini, is reportedly in the works as well.

For anyone that isn’t aware, what is Eat The Rich about?

It’s an autobiographical one-woman show about a young woman from Liverpool who gets a place at Cambridge. She’s super excited. She feels like she deserves a place there. Then she is confronted with a lot of class-based prejudice she wasn’t expecting. She gets a job there, which isn’t allowed, so she has to live this double life, hiding the fact that she is a cleaner from her friends and boyfriend, while also earning money to keep up with the lavish lifestyle of everyone else.

You say it is autobiographical. How true is it?

I say that it is 90 per-cent true. I went to Cambridge. I was a cleaner. I had a boyfriend that went to Eton. He is coming to see the show next week actually. It’s okay. He is an actor. I’ve promised him a role in the Netflix show if it happens.

What about the version of you that you play? How accurate is that?

I was a bit older when I went to Cambridge than I present in the play. In the play I’ve only taken one year out. In reality, I took four. I’d been getting into drama schools but not able to go because I didn’t have the finances to fund it, so I was a bit older and I was aware of systemic barriers around education. I actually went to study education and theatre at Cambridge because I wanted to change that. I was surprised by the classism at Cambridge, though. I didn’t expect people to say awful things about Liverpool to my face. I didn’t expect people to take the piss out of my accent. I didn’t expect people to make assumptions about me in the way that they did. I didn’t expect them to be so bold about it. There are some horrific things I either experienced first-hand, or heard through other students from similar backgrounds, but chose not to put in the show, partly because it would have been too obvious who it was about and I didn’t want any Baby Reindeer lawsuits.

The show was a big hit at the Edinburgh Fringe. Why do you think people loved it?

There’s a few things. I think people love an underdog story, regardless of class or race or background. I think that is why the show has been compared to so many other stories, even ones that I haven’t even read. I also think that class is something people still feel like they can’t really talk about. We don’t have the same language or tools to talk about class in the way that we do race or gender or sexuality, and I think the play opens up a space for that conversation. And, beyond that, I think it allows people to connect with the experience of being othered, whether that is because they are working class or black or trans or gay or whatever. There’s some kind of Venn diagram going on that I’ll probably post on Instagram at some point.

The show is really funny but there are some serious points amid that humour, too.

Definitely. Some of the earlier drafts had a lot of anger and viciousness in them, which I had to get out because it is something I really feel. A lot of my audience, at the Fringe and at Soho Theatre, will be from a middle-class background, though. A lot of them will have connections to institutions like Cambridge. They are not going to listen to me if I’m just shouting at them. If I set stuff up as a joke, though, then drop something in about class or JK Rowling or genocide, and there’s a moment of awkward silence, then I quickly move on, I hope that makes people think more. I’ve snuck it in the back door. People can get quite defensive about identity, but you can get through to them if you’re taking the piss. That’s how I connect with men of a certain generation. I thought I’d apply that to audiences.

You had to scramble to get Eat The Rich on after some funding fell through, right?

That’s right. I had a relationship with a West End producer, who I won’t name but they know who they are, and they had been really supportive of the show from a really early sharing, right up until the month before we were going to Edinburgh, at which point we had budgeted for between £10,000 and £15,000 from them. We thought that money was coming, then all of a sudden it wasn’t. We were like: ‘Shit, we have to crowdfund, otherwise this isn’t going to happen.’ Somehow, we managed to raise £10,000. That is testament to the nice, rich friends I made at Cambridge, and also to, like, girls from school who I haven’t spoken to for ten years who chipped in with £5. That was a very stressful period, but I think it actually ended up helping the show because it created an underdog story around it.

You totally sold out in Edinburgh, added extra shows, and won a Fringe First. It must have been an incredible month.

It was amazing. I was also, like, massively overwhelmed. Our pre-sales were already quite good when we got there as we’d done a lot of prep and emailing. The first preview was shocking. It was so bad. I am so embarrassed that some of my friends came to it. The first show sold out, but only because I had my auntie up and she was out on the mile, with her rollers in, handing out flyers with me and being hilarious. I have a bit of the gift of the gab, too, and I think as soon as people heard ‘Cambridge Footlights’ and my accent, they were interested. But it was Mark Fisher’s review in The Guardian, without a doubt, that did it. We went in to see another show having sold out the following day. When we came out, I checked my phone and we’d sold out every single show for the rest of the run.

And now you are performing the show at Soho Theatre. Was that always the plan?

That only happened after the Fringe. They came to see it and offered us a slot, which was amazing. The show has changed quite a bit. None of the text has changed, but we have levelled up the sound and lighting and stuff.

And it has been reported that you are turning Eat The Rich into a Netflix show.

That was leaked to Deadline. I don’t know what little rat told them. I’ve kept it to myself because I’ve not signed anything yet and I’m scared it’s going to get taken away from me. It’s been such a dream of mine for so long and it feels like it is happening, but that anxiety is only exacerbated by Deadline running articles with my face and people sharing it, and me having to put something on Instagram as a result. But, yeah, it is in development with Netflix and Phil Barantini, who has been great, and another production company that Deadline didn’t hear about. They are going to do a proper announcement in the next few weeks, I think.

The success of Eat The Rich has been compared to the success of other Edinburgh Fringe shows, particularly Fleabag and Baby Reindeer. How do you feel about that?

It’s lovely because I’ve got massive respect for those artists, although I find the Fleabag one a bit confusing because I’ve never seen Fleabag and, other than the Fringe-to-TV thing, I don’t get what the comparison is. I try not to think about it too much, though, because it’s a huge weight to bear and I’d probably be quite stressed if I did. I do find it interesting, and I hope this isn’t taken in the wrong way, that people like Jack Rooke and Michaela Coel, who have also had massive success from Fringe to TV and are both working class, are never mentioned when we are talking about these pinnacles of success. Jack donated a large amount of money to the fundraiser before he knew who I was or seen the show. He came to see the show in Edinburgh and has been really helpful with all the TV stuff since. I’ve been overwhelmed by his kindness, and I think it is a shame that people never reference working-class artists like him that have had a similar journey to Fleabag. I’m more comfortable being compared to people like Jack and Michaela.

You are writing a play for the Everyman and Playhouse, too. What is that about?

I love reality TV. I’m kind of obsessed with it, so I am writing a play that has a similar set up to shows like The Traitors, Love Is Blind, and Married At First Sight, where the producers are really pulling the strings. It is set in the early 2000s, so it’s a bit more fucked up than reality TV now, and the premise is that a girl has died and all these people come in to see her, and it turns out they’re all her ex-boyfriends. It’s all crushed velvet and golden goblets. Then, it turns out she’s not really dead and she just wanted to hear what they never said in the relationship. It’s an exploration of grief and of how we talk about people with rose-tinted glasses when they die, especially ex-partners. I’ve only written half of it. I need to finish it.

Where did you grow up?

I’m from Wallasey on the Wirral. The politics of that are interesting. Some Scousers are like: ‘You’re a wool. You don’t have a purple bin.’ And I’m like: ‘I live ten minutes away from town and worked in Liverpool for six years and went to LIPA as a kid and have a Liverpool season ticket.’ At Cambridge, I tell people I’m from Liverpool because they don’t know where the Wirral is, then I meet a real Scouser and they tell me I can’t say that. I’m in this weird in-between place where I can’t win, but I’m really proud of Liverpool and proud of Merseyside.

What do you think of Arne Slot? Do you think he was riding on the coattails of Klopp when Liverpool won the title last year?

I think he was riding on the coattails of Klopp, if I’m being completely honest. I think it was all Salah’s doing. Salah can do no wrong in my eyes.

How did you get into Cambridge? Is the way you depict it in Eat The Rich true?

The reality was that I literally emailed, like, Cambridge.com when I was drunk saying I’d got these A-levels and I was interested in the education course. Six months later, this guy called Jonathan emailed me back and was like: ‘You’ve got the right grades and with your school and postcode, you contextually stand a good chance of getting in.’ He paid for me to go down twice on the train, once with my mum, and I basically got in though him holding my hand. That was actually the part of the play I struggled with most because I was at Cambridge because of him, but I didn’t want it to seem like you have to be babied through the process if you’re working class, because I don’t think that’s true, and it does a disservice to everyone else from a working-class background. Does that make sense?

You became president of the Cambridge Footlights. How did that come about?

I did a lot of stand-up at Cambridge. I was 22 and quite sure of myself. I auditioned for a lot of shows and knew I was a good actor, and was like: ‘Why are these 18-year-old directors telling me I’m rubbish and can’t do Shakespeare?’ So I was like: ‘Fuck it, I’ll just tell my own stories.’ That’s where Eat The Rich came from. That’s how I ended up knowing people in Footlights, too. I wasn’t deemed funny enough to ever actually be a member, one of the cohort of comedians, but I became the president because I was good at producing. I produced and directed a lot. It was a huge part of my life. Covid fucked everything up, though. I think that famous trajectory from Cambridge Footlights to successful comedian has taken a lot longer for my year group because we came into an industry that was fucked because of Covid.

You worked at the Royal Court for a while after you graduated, right?

I worked as an admin assistant, then worked my way up to the education team, doing participation work. I was made redundant in the big cull when David Byrne took over, though, as was Ellie Fulcher, my dramaturg, and Jasmyn Fisher-Ryner, my producer. I thank the man now, though, because I don’t know what would have happened if we hadn’t been forced into that unfortunate situation.

And you still work in outreach alongside your creative stuff, too?

I do. I was at the Royal Court when For Black Boys was first brought there from the New Diorama by Jasmyn. She saw it at the New Diorama, took it to Vicky, and Vicky said she should produce it, and that was the beginning of her career. She went on to produce it in the West End as well. I was attached to that show as outreach coordinator. I was basically responsible for figuring how to get black audiences into the Royal Court and the West End. There was a big strategy involving social media and cold calling community groups and convincing people theatre was cool. Anyway, I made loads of different contacts through that, so when I was made redundant, I thought I should do something with that. I had all these people who were suddenly really excited by theatre and pestering me about seeing other shows, so I set up my own little consultancy company, supporting mostly West End producers with audience development. I moved back to Liverpool, but I can just do it all remotely. At the moment I’m working on All My Sons and Deep Azure. I call myself a theatre plug. I plug people into theatre as and where I can.

How does that process work?

I encourage theatres to have a certain amount of premium seats available between free and £15 for every show, then I basically process the admin behind who gets those seats, whether it is schools, community groups, charities, whatever. This year, I would like to set up a system that theatres can opt into and people can log onto and do it that way. There is a need for more diverse stories on stage, particularly in the West End, and that comes with a responsibility to invite diverse audiences. There are some amazing advocates like Ryan Calais Cameron and Paapa Essiedu, but it should not just be on the black writers and actors to put that in place. Producers should be thinking about that anyway, not just to appease the star.

Eat The Rich (but maybe not me mates x) is at Soho Theatre until January 31.

Three shows to see next week

Guess How Much I Love You? - Royal Court Theatre, until February 21

The Royal Court Theatre’s first show of 2026 looks fantastic. Fourteen years on from his acclaimed debut Goodbye To All That, playwright Luke Norris returns to the Sloane Square venue with Guess How Much I Love You?, a three-handed drama about a pregnant couple, impossible choices and enduring love. Directed by surefooted former Headlong boss Jeremy Herrin, it stars the remarkable Welsh actor Rosie Sheehy – superb in Romeo and Julie, Machinal, The Brightening Air, and more – alongside Robert Aramayo and Lena Kaur, and runs in the Jerwood Theatre Downstairs until late February. You can get tickets via the button below.

This is promotional content.

Book tickets here

A Ghost In Your Ear - Hampstead Theatre, until January 31

Rosie Sheehy also starred in the London production of Jamie Armitage’s compelling Edinburgh Fringe thriller An Interrogation last year. Now, Armitage is back at Hampstead Theatre Downstairs with A Ghost In Your Ear, a new horror play conceived with sound designer brothers Ben and Max Ringham, which incorporates binaural audio and stars George Blagden as a young actor recording a spooky audiobook. The critics were thoroughly creeped out. You can get tickets via the button below.

Book tickets here

Out In The Hills - Pitlochry Festival Theatre, until January 18

It is not a show exactly, but Alan Cumming’s revolutionary regime at Pitlochry Festival Theatre kicks off next weekend, when the Perthshire venue hosts a new LGBTQIA+ festival featuring a host of big names. There will be conversations with Graham Norton, Jackie Kay, Russell T Davis, and others; a performance of storyteller Niall Moorjani’s The Green Knight (but it’s gay); rehearsed readings starring Ian McKellen and Cumming himself; and more. You can get tickets via the button below.

Book tickets here

This year, I would like to set up a system that theatres can opt into and people can log onto and do it that way. There is a need for more diverse stories on stage, particularly in the West End, and that comes with a responsibility to invite diverse audiences. There are some amazing advocates like Ryan Calais Cameron and Paapa Essiedu, but it should not just be on the black writers and actors to put that in place. Producers should be thinking about that anyway, not just to appease the star. Eat The Rich (but maybe not me mates x) is at Soho Theatre until January 31. Help keep The Crush Bar going by signing up as a paid supporter for £5/month or £50/year, or a champion supporter for £250/year. The Royal Court Theatre’s first show of 2026 looks fantastic. Fourteen years on from his acclaimed debut Goodbye To All That, playwright Luke Norris returns to the Sloane Square venue with Guess How Much I Love You?, a three-handed drama about a pregnant couple, impossible choices and enduring love. Directed by surefooted former Headlong boss Jeremy Herrin, it stars the remarkable Welsh actor Rosie Sheehy – superb in Romeo and Julie, Machinal, The Brightening Air, and more – alongside Robert Aramayo and Lena Kaur, and runs in the Jerwood Theatre Downstairs until late February. You can get tickets via the button below. This is promotional content. Book tickets here Rosie Sheehy also starred in the London production of Jamie Armitage’s compelling Edinburgh Fringe thriller An Interrogation last year. Now, Armitage is back at Hampstead Theatre Downstairs with A Ghost In Your Ear, a new horror play conceived with sound designer brothers Ben and Max Ringham, which incorporates binaural audio and stars George Blagden as a young actor recording a spooky audiobook. The critics were thoroughly creeped out. You can get tickets via the button below. Book tickets here It is not a show exactly, but Alan Cumming’s revolutionary regime at Pitlochry Festival Theatre kicks off next weekend, when the Perthshire venue hosts a new LGBTQIA+ festival featuring a host of big names. There will be conversations with Graham Norton, Jackie Kay, Russell T Davis, and others; a performance of storyteller Niall Moorjani’s The Green Knight (but it’s gay); rehearsed readings starring Ian McKellen and Cumming himself; and more. You can get tickets via the button below. Book tickets here Help keep The Crush Bar going by signing up as a paid supporter for £5/month or £50/year, or a champion supporter for £250/year. Thanks to all 4927 subscribers, to all 152 paid supporters, and particularly to The Crush Bar’s champion supporters, whose contributions make all this possible. Subscribe now That is it for this week. If you want to get in touch about anything raised in this issue - or anything at all - just reply to this newsletter. Fergus

title: The Crush Bar's End-Of-Year Lists
author: thecrushbar
raw_article:
Hello, and welcome to The Crush Bar, a newsletter about theatre by Fergus Morgan.

This is the free Friday issue, which usually contains an interview with an exciting theatremaker or an essay on a theatre-related topic. This week, though, as it is the final issue of the year, it features twelve - too many! - different end-of-year lists.

In case you missed it, here is this week’s issue of Shouts and Murmurs, which is a weekly round-up of the most interesting writing about theatre elsewhere…

You can get Shouts and Murmurs straight in your inbox every Tuesday - and help keep this newsletter going! - by signing up as a paid supporter of The Crush Bar below.

Theatres, companies, agencies and other organisations can also sign-up as champion supporters of The Crush Bar, which gets their name in this dedicated tab on The Crush Bar’s Substack homepage and in the footer of every Friday issue.

Subscribe now

Welcome to the final issue of the The Crush Bar this year.

Sure, I could have found someone interesting to interview. Yes, I could have written a long and thoughtful examination of the industry’s evolution over the last twelve months. Yes, I could have simply left your inbox alone. But why would I, when I can write a load of lists? Lists of good shows! Lists of bad shows! Big shows! Small shows! Some as big as your head! Lists of newsletters! Lists of gifts! Lists of lists! Lists²!

In an effort to show some imagination and distinguish my lists from everyone else’s lists, I have decided to structure mine like The Twelve Days of Christmas: twelve drummers drumming, eleven pipers piping, etc. Singing is optional, but encouraged.

Twelve shows I saw and loved

I catch quite a strange array of work: almost everything produced in Scotland, an eclectic selection of stuff in London, and a whole heap of random shows at the Edinburgh Fringe. Here are my twelve favourites from the first two categories…

The Years, West End
Again, technically this premiered last summer, but I saw it in February. Forget all the nonsense about trigger warnings: Eline Arbo’s multi-voiced reworking of Annie Ernaux’s memoir is one of the theatrical achievements of the decade.

Confessions of a Shinagawa Monkey, Tramway
Vanishing Point’s bilingual adaptation of two Haruki Murakami short stories was compellingly ambiguous and brilliantly slick, and featured perhaps the finest primate performance ever seen on a Scottish stage from Sandy Grierson.

The Weir, West End
Amazingly, this was my first time seeing Conor McPherson’s contemporary classic about old friends swapping stories in an Irish pub. Maybe it should be my last, too, as I cannot imagine it being performed better than by Brendan Gleeson and co.

Water Colour, Pitlochry Festival Theatre
Milly Sweeney’s debut orbited around two young Glaswegians suffering mental health crises. It was deftly structured and deeply moving, earning Sweeney one of The Stage Debut Awards, and it deserves a future life elsewhere.

Stereophonic, West End
The most Tony-nominated play of all time did not disappoint. David Adjmi’s durational depiction of Fleetwood Mac an unnamed British-American band recording Rumours a seminal album over several sessions was like an Annie Baker play with added rock songs. When everything clicked musically, it was transcendent.

Small Acts Of Love, Citizens Theatre
Glasgow’s Citizens Theatre finally reopened after a long-delayed but undeniably stunning redevelopment with Frances Poet and Ricky Ross’s sensitive, kaleidoscopic play-with-songs about the aftermath of the Lockerbie bombing.

Punch, Young Vic
Technically, James Graham’s drama about restorative justice premiered in Nottingham in 2024, but I saw it at the Young Vic in March, so I’m including it. Everything people say is true: it is not his tightest work, but it might well be his most moving, with David Shields supplying a sensationally animated performance.

Arlington, Tron Theatre
Glasgow-based company Shotput’s staging of Enda Walsh’s dystopian 2016 dance-theatre triptych was strange, slick, and exactly the kind of thing that Scottish theatre should do more often.

The Seagull, Barbican
Thomas Ostermeier’s take on Chekhov featured electric guitars, quad-bikes, a histrionic Cate Blanchett, and an exquisite use of The Stranglers’ Golden Brown. Some hated it. I loved it. Edinburgh’s answer was disappointingly trad in comparison.

The Lonely Londoners, Kiln Theatre
Again, it first ran in 2024, but I caught Ebenezer Bamgboye and Roy Williams’ exquisite, painful and profound adaptation of Sam Selvon’s Windrush novel in its expanded version at the Kiln Theatre in February, so I am including it.

The Line Of Beauty, Almeida Theatre
Not everyone liked Jack Holden’s adaptation of Alan Hollinghurst’s Brideshead-ish novel about Thatcher’s Britain and the AIDS crisis, but I thought it was astute and entertaining, and that Michael Grandage’s production was sheer elegance.

The Lady From The Sea, Bridge Theatre
I have been a fan of Simon Stone ever since his extraordinary Medea with Marieke Heebink. His update of Ibsen’s story lacked the same visceral power - see also: Yerma with Billie Piper - but it did have Andrew Lincoln in a swimming pool.

Oh, and if you want a reminder of my top ten Edinburgh Fringe shows, here you go…

Eleven shows everyone else enjoyed

Sadly, my time, finances and connections are limited resources, and there were plenty of amazing shows I missed. Here are eleven that will pop up on everyone else’s lists…

Titanique, West End
Layton Williams playing an iceberg thawed even the grumpiest critics.

Much Ado About Nothing, West End
A dad-dancing Tom Hiddleston helped Jamie Lloyd bounce back from The Tempest.

The Brightening Air, Old Vic
Chris O’Dowd was excellent in Conor McPherson’s umpteenth opening of the year.

All My Sons, West End
Eleven years after A View From The Bridge, Ivo van Hove does it again with Miller.

Inter Alia, National Theatre
Rosamund Pike impressed in Suzie Miller’s West End-bound follow-up to Prima Facie.

Evita, West End
Rachel Zegler singing on the balcony of the Palladium was the stunt of the year.

Intimate Apparel, Donmar Warehouse
Lynette Linton directing Lynn Nottage plays at the Donmar just works.

Dealer’s Choice, Donmar Warehouse
Likewise with Patrick Marber’s poker play and director Matthew Dunster. A great fit.

Cyrano De Bergerac, RSC
Adrian Lester was the poet with the proboscis in Simon Evans’ Stratford staging.

Paddington The Musical, West End
Of course, they made a Paddington musical and, of course, everyone loved it.

Ghosts, Lyric Hammersmith
Gary Owen and Rachel O’Riordan reunited for this acclaimed twist on Ibsen.

And, I can’t be sure, but I expect Into The Woods, The Playboy Of The Western World, Christmas Day, Paranormal Activity and Oh, Mary! will feature on several lists as well.

Special mentions: Girls and Boys at Nottingham Playhouse, A Good House at the Royal Court, Measure For Measure at the RSC, Abigail’s Party at the Royal Exchange Theatre, Emma at the Rose Theatre, Animal Farm at Theatre Royal Stratford East.

Ten shows to look forward to in 2026

There is always next year. Here are a bunch of forthcoming productions that sit in the centre of my strange Venn diagram, and that I am excited to see…

Arcadia, Old Vic, from 24 January
The late, great Tom Stoppard’s 1993 masterpiece, directed by Carrie Cracknell.

John Proctor Is The Villain, Royal Court Theatre, from 20 March
Kimberley Bellflower’s high school spin on The Crucible was a big hit in New York.

Dracula, West End, from 4 February
Australian auteur Kip Williams and Cynthia Erivo do the Dorian Gray thing.

1536, West End, from 2 May
Ava Pickett’s anachronistic Tudor drama transfers from the Almeida Theatre.

A Trial, Edinburgh International Festival, from 7 August
Christiane Jatahy directs Wagner Moura in this historic international co-pro.

Sweat, Citizens Theatre, from 2 May
Rising star Joanna Bowman stages the Scottish premiere of Lynn Nottage’s play.

Ceilidh, Pitlochry Festival Theatre, from 24 September
Musical duo Noisemaker’s new show will be directed by Tony-winner Sam Pinkleton.

A Doll’s House, Almeida Theatre, from 31 March
Iconoclastic director Joe Hill-Gibbins assaults Ibsen’s classic play in Islington.

Fun Home, Royal Exchange Theatre, from 4 July
Eight years later, and we finally we get another version of the Tony-winning musical.

Bonus picks: Ballad Lines at Southwark Playhouse, Deep Azure at Shakespeare’s Globe, Aether at Jermyn Street Theatre, The Virgins at Soho Theatre.

Nine shows that divided opinion

I spend an inordinate amount of time reading other people’s reviews. Here are nine shows that split the critics, usually because they contained handheld microphones…

Romans: A Novel, Almeida Theatre
Alice Birch’s new play was a five-star, “era-spanning, heart-shattering exploration of the male experience” for WhatsOnStage’s Lucinda Everett, but a one-star dud with “nothing original to say” for The Times’ Clive Davis.

The Maids, Donmar Warehouse
Kip Williams’ screen-heavy take on Jean Genet was “overwhelming”, “gross” and “brilliant” for The Stage’s Sam Marlowe, who gave it five stars, but The Express’ Stefan Kyriazis found it “almost unwatchable” and gave it one star.

Bacchae, National Theatre
Indhu Rubasingham’s first show as artistic director of the NT was “rude, rhyming, scatalogical and utterly gripping” for WhatsOnStage’s Sarah Crompton, but a one-star “travesty” for Davis in The Times.

Hamlet, National Theatre
A few days later, and Hiran Abeysekera’s Dane had “febrile humour, nihilistic energy, and unsettling volatility” for The Financial Times’ Sarah Hemming, but was “more stand-up comedian than prince” according to The Stage’s Dave Fargnoli.

Mary Page Marlowe, Old Vic
Tracy Letts’ life-spanning drama was deemed “quietly affecting and deeply poignant” by LondonTheatre’s Aliya Al-Hassan, but Time Out’s Andrzej Lukowski objected to its “fussy theatricality.”

Juniper Blood, Donmar Warehouse
WhatsOnStage’s Crompton thought Mike Bartlett’s rural play was “supremely funny” and “utterly absorbing”, but The Standard’s Nick Curtis dismissed it as “thin and undercooked” with “underwritten” characters” and “screeds” instead of dialogue.

Manhunt, Royal Court Theatre
Rob Icke’s play was “a compellingly weird examination of toxic masculinity” for Time Out’s Lukowski, but The Independent’s Alice Saville thought it lacked “Icke’s usual ability to winnow complex source material into one coherent message.”

Hamlet, RSC
Rupert Goold’s Titanic take on Shakespeare’s tragedy was “eerie, nightmarish and claustrophobic” for Hemming in The FT, but The Stage’s Holly O’Mahoney objected to star Luke Thallon’s “long pauses and mono-tonal outpourings.”

Make It Happen, Edinburgh International Festival
James Graham’s banking drama was a superb showcase of his ability to explain “great sweeps of history in highly entertaining ways” according to Tim Bano in The Financial Times, but I thought it lacked his “trademark clarity” in The Stage.

Eight excellent newsletters

I thought about listing The Crush Bar’s most-read newsletters of 2025, but my recent missives have not had time to accrue enough views to compete with instalments from earlier in the year, so here is simply a selection of my favourites…

In January, I chatted to director Ebenezer Bamgboye about his extraordinary adaptation of The Lonely Londoners, his job at Southwark Playhouse, and more…

Later that month, I went to see A Complete Unknown in the cinema, felt sad that I didn’t live in New York in the 1960s, and wrote about that…

In February, I spoke to Jay Miller, artistic director of Hackney Wick’s trailblazing Yard Theatre, ahead of it being torn down and rebuilt…

In April, I surveyed my fellow critics to find out their favourite uses of songs in theatre, inspired by Thomas Ostermeier’s use of The Stranglers in The Seagull…

In May, rising star Joanna Bowman talked to me about her striking staging of Escaped Alone, her directorial debut with the RSC, and more…

Ahead of the Edinburgh Fringe, I got loads of different people to send me their best advice for surviving and thriving at the festival, and stuck it all in a newsletter…

In October, I had some deep thoughts about theatre’s place in a society addled my smartphones, social media, and short-form video content…

Last week, inspired by that symposium on theatre criticism I went to in Dublin, I thought it would be nice if we found out a bit more about our leading critics…

Seven other bits of work I’m proud of

I don’t just write here in The Crush Bar, you know? Oh no, I contribute insightful and entertaining journalism to a wide range of publications. Here is some…

I interviewed dozens of famous names for my weekly interview slot in The Stage, including Aisling Loftus, Ivo Graham, David Shields, Charlie Stemp, Joshua McGuire, Freema Agyeman, Kate Fleetwood, Vicky Featherstone, Brian Cox, Khalid Abdalla, Kyle Soller, Andy Nyman, Sam Heughan, and Alexandra Burke. My favourite, though, was always going to be my chat with national treasure Miriam Margolyes.

“I think I’m a much better actress than most people think I am. People think I’m quite good, but I think I could have been quite wonderful if I’d had the chance.”

The Financial Times let me write for them again in August. I reviewed She’s Behind You and Lost Lear at the Traverse Theatre. I hope they let me write for them again!

“It is an astonishing piece of theatre — intelligently constructed, imaginatively staged and devastatingly moving.”

Also in August, I returned to BBC Radio 4’s Front Row, after being snubbed in 2024, and chatted about Edinburgh Fringe shows with Kirsty Wark and Neil Cooper…

Prospect Magazine asked me to do a bit for them on Michael Sheen starting the Welsh National Theatre. Sadly, it is not available online, but here’s a taste…

“The real hope is not that Sheen can single-handedly save Wales from cultural oblivion, but that he can set an example. Underneath the misty-eyed patriotism evoked by his stirring orations is something else: the refreshing sight of a big star genuinely putting his time, effort and money where his mouth is for once.”

I also wrote a few bigger interviews and long-reads for The Stage this year. The best was probably my chat with Luke Thallon, ahead of him playing Hamlet for the RSC. We nerded out over Rob Icke’s Uncle Vanya and Carrie Cracknell’s The Deep Blue Sea…

“Isn’t it amazing that shows like that can happen? Shows like that come along and bite chunks out of us and then disappear, but we are still talking about them today. I love this about theatre. It infuriates me and confuses me, but I love it.”

In March, the monthly interview I wrote for Stage Directors UK became a podcast. The first episode was a chat with the RSC’s Tamara Harvey and the Tron Theatre’s Jemima Levick on the fraught process of taking over an organisation…

Lastly, in what I am now realising is quite a solipsistic section of this newsletter, I chaired the drama judging panels for the inaugural British Audio Awards, giving some much-deserved critical attention to some genuinely terrific work.

Help keep The Crush Bar going by signing up as a paid supporter for £5/month or £50/year, or a champion supporter for £250/year.

Six talking points from 2025

As ever, the theatre world was alive with disagreements and debate, clashes and culture wars in 2025. Luckily, you had The Crush Bar to guide you through it all…

In February, a spate of fainting during the West End run of The Years resulted in the tiresome discourse around trigger warnings flaring up like a recurring infection…

In March, an independent review into the cancellation of A Midsummer Night’s Dream at the Royal Exchange Theatre was released, sparking a debate about censorship, artistic leadership, and whether inserting rap in Shakespeare is ever a good idea…

Plenty of famous names took to The Stage this year - Rami Malek in Oedipus, Brie Larson in Electra, Tom Hiddleston in Much Ado About Nothing, Jonathan Bailey in Richard II, Ewan McGregor in My Master Builder - while Alan Cumming also took over Pitlochry Festival Theatre and Michael Sheen started the Welsh National Theatre…

Clueless, Hercules, The Hunger Games, 50 First Dates, Starter For Ten, 13 Going On 30, Freaky Friday… the list of unimaginative screen-to-stage adaptations grew even longer this year, with one mammalian musical coming into my crosshairs in particular…

Indhu Rubasingham took over as artistic director of the National Theatre this year, unveiling her first shows in May. It will be fascinating to see how her tenure unfolds…

Caryl Churchill pulling a play from the Donmar Warehouse and Samuel West and Siobhán McSweeney starring in a screened play outside the Old Vic were two high-profile protests against unethical corporate sponsorship of the arts. As always, the ones calling them childish were the ones afraid of having an adult conversation…

Fiiiiiiiive shows that were full-on flops

It isn’t nice to dwell on failure, particularly at Christmas, so let’s deal with this section quickly. Among a fair few flops this year, the five that stood out were: The Hunger Games On Stage, Electra, Unicorn, The Great Gatsby, and, up here in Scotland, Keli.

Four questions about The Crush Bar

If by some miracle you have made it this far, then you are clearly a committed reader of The Crush Bar, and I would value your opinion on how it evolves next year. If your answers are not represented, please do drop me a line with your thoughts!

Three gift ideas for the theatre person in your life

I really am scraping the barrel with this Twelve Days of Christmas format here…

A subscription to NT at Home. I think the National Theatre’s streaming service is hugely underrated. For just £9.99/month - and there are decent discounts available - you can access loads of seminal productions, including Present Laughter with Andrew Scott, A View From The Bridge with Mark Strong, and Angels In America with Andrew Garfield. New stuff is constantly being added, too: Next To Normal, The Estate, The Brightening Air, The Land Of The Living, Bacchae, and more…

A playtext subscription. For £110, Nick Hern Books will send you or your loved one a physical copy of a newly published script. Apparently, it could be “a major premiere from a big-name playwright, a timely new version of a classic, or a fresh and exciting work by a talented, up-and-coming writer.” Other options are available, too.

A ticket to something good. If they like theatre, maybe just get them a theatre ticket? Or, if none of those ideas work, then take a look at this list I published last December…

Two bits of important info

Firstly, as mentioned above, this is the final issue of The Crush Bar in 2025. It will be back in your inboxes in early January, but only for a few weeks because…

Secondly, my wife and I are off travelling for two months in February and March, so The Crush Bar will be on hiatus. Yes, I will pause payments. Yes, I am terrified!

One last chance to become a paid supporter

We made it. Wow. What a comprehensive and entertaining dash through the year in theatre. Surely - surely - content like this is worth sustaining for a few quid a month.

Help keep The Crush Bar going by signing up as a paid supporter for £5/month or £50/year, or a champion supporter for £250/year.

Thanks for reading and supporting The Crush Bar

Thanks to all 4875 subscribers, to all 149 paid supporters, and particularly to The Crush Bar’s champion supporters, whose contributions make all this possible.

The Royal Court Theatre, Francesca Moody Productions, Raw Material Arts, Jermyn Street Theatre; Hampstead Theatre; Storytelling PR; Premier Scotland; Ellie Keel Productions; The Women’s Prize For Playwriting

Your organisation can become a champion supporter of The Crush Bar, join that list, and help keep its independent coverage of theatre going via the button below.

Subscribe now

That is it for this week. If you want to get in touch about anything raised in this issue - or anything at all - just reply to this newsletter. Happy Christmas.

Fergus
