I spend an inordinate amount of time reading other people’s reviews. Here are nine shows that split the critics, usually because they contained handheld microphones… Romans: A Novel, Almeida TheatreAlice Birch’s new play was a five-star, “era-spanning, heart-shattering exploration of the male experience” for WhatsOnStage’s Lucinda Everett, but a one-star dud with “nothing original to say” for The Times’ Clive Davis. The Maids, Donmar WarehouseKip Williams’ screen-heavy take on Jean Genet was “overwhelming”, “gross” and “brilliant” for The Stage’s Sam Marlowe, who gave it five stars, but The Express’ Stefan Kyriazis found it “almost unwatchable” and gave it one star. Bacchae, National TheatreIndhu Rubasingham’s first show as artistic director of the NT was “rude, rhyming, scatalogical and utterly gripping” for WhatsOnStage’s Sarah Crompton, but a one-star “travesty” for Davis in The Times. Hamlet, National TheatreA few days later, and Hiran Abeysekera’s Dane had “febrile humour, nihilistic energy, and unsettling volatility” for The Financial Times’ Sarah Hemming, but was “more stand-up comedian than prince” according to The Stage’s Dave Fargnoli. Mary Page Marlowe, Old VicTracy Letts’ life-spanning drama was deemed “quietly affecting and deeply poignant” by LondonTheatre’s Aliya Al-Hassan, but Time Out’s Andrzej Lukowski objected to its “fussy theatricality.” Juniper Blood, Donmar WarehouseWhatsOnStage’s Crompton thought Mike Bartlett’s rural play was “supremely funny” and “utterly absorbing”, but The Standard’s Nick Curtis dismissed it as “thin and undercooked” with “underwritten” characters” and “screeds” instead of dialogue. Manhunt, Royal Court TheatreRob Icke’s play was “a compellingly weird examination of toxic masculinity” for Time Out’s Lukowski, but The Independent’s Alice Saville thought it lacked “Icke’s usual ability to winnow complex source material into one coherent message.” Hamlet, RSCRupert Goold’s Titanic take on Shakespeare’s tragedy was “eerie, nightmarish and claustrophobic” for Hemming in The FT, but The Stage’s Holly O’Mahoney objected to star Luke Thallon’s “long pauses and mono-tonal outpourings.” Make It Happen, Edinburgh International FestivalJames Graham’s banking drama was a superb showcase of his ability to explain “great sweeps of history in highly entertaining ways” according to Tim Bano in The Financial Times, but I thought it lacked his “trademark clarity” in The Stage. I thought about listing The Crush Bar’s most-read newsletters of 2025, but my recent missives have not had time to accrue enough views to compete with instalments from earlier in the year, so here is simply a selection of my favourites… In January, I chatted to director Ebenezer Bamgboye about his extraordinary adaptation of The Lonely Londoners, his job at Southwark Playhouse, and more… Later that month, I went to see A Complete Unknown in the cinema, felt sad that I didn’t live in New York in the 1960s, and wrote about that… In February, I spoke to Jay Miller, artistic director of Hackney Wick’s trailblazing Yard Theatre, ahead of it being torn down and rebuilt… In April, I surveyed my fellow critics to find out their favourite uses of songs in theatre, inspired by Thomas Ostermeier’s use of The Stranglers in The Seagull… In May, rising star Joanna Bowman talked to me about her striking staging of Escaped Alone, her directorial debut with the RSC, and more… Ahead of the Edinburgh Fringe, I got loads of different people to send me their best advice for surviving and thriving at the festival, and stuck it all in a newsletter… In October, I had some deep thoughts about theatre’s place in a society addled my smartphones, social media, and short-form video content… Last week, inspired by that symposium on theatre criticism I went to in Dublin, I thought it would be nice if we found out a bit more about our leading critics… I don’t just write here in The Crush Bar, you know? Oh no, I contribute insightful and entertaining journalism to a wide range of publications. Here is some… I interviewed dozens of famous names for my weekly interview slot in The Stage, including Aisling Loftus, Ivo Graham, David Shields, Charlie Stemp, Joshua McGuire, Freema Agyeman, Kate Fleetwood, Vicky Featherstone, Brian Cox, Khalid Abdalla, Kyle Soller, Andy Nyman, Sam Heughan, and Alexandra Burke. My favourite, though, was always going to be my chat with national treasure Miriam Margolyes. “I think I’m a much better actress than most people think I am. People think I’m quite good, but I think I could have been quite wonderful if I’d had the chance.” The Financial Times let me write for them again in August. I reviewed She’s Behind You and Lost Lear at the Traverse Theatre. I hope they let me write for them again! “It is an astonishing piece of theatre — intelligently constructed, imaginatively staged and devastatingly moving.” Also in August, I returned to BBC Radio 4’s Front Row, after being snubbed in 2024, and chatted about Edinburgh Fringe shows with Kirsty Wark and Neil Cooper… Prospect Magazine asked me to do a bit for them on Michael Sheen starting the Welsh National Theatre. Sadly, it is not available online, but here’s a taste… “The real hope is not that Sheen can single-handedly save Wales from cultural oblivion, but that he can set an example. Underneath the misty-eyed patriotism evoked by his stirring orations is something else: the refreshing sight of a big star genuinely putting his time, effort and money where his mouth is for once.” I also wrote a few bigger interviews and long-reads for The Stage this year. The best was probably my chat with Luke Thallon, ahead of him playing Hamlet for the RSC. We nerded out over Rob Icke’s Uncle Vanya and Carrie Cracknell’s The Deep Blue Sea… “Isn’t it amazing that shows like that can happen? Shows like that come along and bite chunks out of us and then disappear, but we are still talking about them today. I love this about theatre. It infuriates me and confuses me, but I love it.” In March, the monthly interview I wrote for Stage Directors UK became a podcast. The first episode was a chat with the RSC’s Tamara Harvey and the Tron Theatre’s Jemima Levick on the fraught process of taking over an organisation… Lastly, in what I am now realising is quite a solipsistic section of this newsletter, I chaired the drama judging panels for the inaugural British Audio Awards, giving some much-deserved critical attention to some genuinely terrific work. Help keep The Crush Bar going by signing up as a paid supporter for £5/month or £50/year, or a champion supporter for £250/year. As ever, the theatre world was alive with disagreements and debate, clashes and culture wars in 2025. Luckily, you had The Crush Bar to guide you through it all… In February, a spate of fainting during the West End run of The Years resulted in the tiresome discourse around trigger warnings flaring up like a recurring infection… In March, an independent review into the cancellation of A Midsummer Night’s Dream at the Royal Exchange Theatre was released, sparking a debate about censorship, artistic leadership, and whether inserting rap in Shakespeare is ever a good idea… Plenty of famous names took to The Stage this year - Rami Malek in Oedipus, Brie Larson in Electra, Tom Hiddleston in Much Ado About Nothing, Jonathan Bailey in Richard II, Ewan McGregor in My Master Builder - while Alan Cumming also took over Pitlochry Festival Theatre and Michael Sheen started the Welsh National Theatre… Clueless, Hercules, The Hunger Games, 50 First Dates, Starter For Ten, 13 Going On 30, Freaky Friday… the list of unimaginative screen-to-stage adaptations grew even longer this year, with one mammalian musical coming into my crosshairs in particular… Indhu Rubasingham took over as artistic director of the National Theatre this year, unveiling her first shows in May. It will be fascinating to see how her tenure unfolds… Caryl Churchill pulling a play from the Donmar Warehouse and Samuel West and Siobhán McSweeney starring in a screened play outside the Old Vic were two high-profile protests against unethical corporate sponsorship of the arts. As always, the ones calling them childish were the ones afraid of having an adult conversation… It isn’t nice to dwell on failure, particularly at Christmas, so let’s deal with this section quickly. Among a fair few flops this year, the five that stood out were: The Hunger Games On Stage, Electra, Unicorn, The Great Gatsby, and, up here in Scotland, Keli. If by some miracle you have made it this far, then you are clearly a committed reader of The Crush Bar, and I would value your opinion on how it evolves next year. If your answers are not represented, please do drop me a line with your thoughts! I really am scraping the barrel with this Twelve Days of Christmas format here… A subscription to NT at Home. I think the National Theatre’s streaming service is hugely underrated. For just £9.99/month - and there are decent discounts available - you can access loads of seminal productions, including Present Laughter with Andrew Scott, A View From The Bridge with Mark Strong, and Angels In America with Andrew Garfield. New stuff is constantly being added, too: Next To Normal, The Estate, The Brightening Air, The Land Of The Living, Bacchae, and more… A playtext subscription. For £110, Nick Hern Books will send you or your loved one a physical copy of a newly published script. Apparently, it could be “a major premiere from a big-name playwright, a timely new version of a classic, or a fresh and exciting work by a talented, up-and-coming writer.” Other options are available, too. A ticket to something good. If they like theatre, maybe just get them a theatre ticket? Or, if none of those ideas work, then take a look at this list I published last December… Firstly, as mentioned above, this is the final issue of The Crush Bar in 2025. It will be back in your inboxes in early January, but only for a few weeks because… Secondly, my wife and I are off travelling for two months in February and March, so The Crush Bar will be on hiatus. Yes, I will pause payments. Yes, I am terrified! We made it. Wow. What a comprehensive and entertaining dash through the year in theatre. Surely - surely - content like this is worth sustaining for a few quid a month. Help keep The Crush Bar going by signing up as a paid supporter for £5/month or £50/year, or a champion supporter for £250/year. Thanks to all 4875 subscribers, to all 149 paid supporters, and particularly to The Crush Bar’s champion supporters, whose contributions make all this possible. The Royal Court Theatre, Francesca Moody Productions, Raw Material Arts, Jermyn Street Theatre; Hampstead Theatre; Storytelling PR; Premier Scotland; Ellie Keel Productions; The Women’s Prize For Playwriting Your organisation can become a champion supporter of The Crush Bar, join that list, and help keep its independent coverage of theatre going via the button below. Subscribe now That is it for this week. If you want to get in touch about anything raised in this issue - or anything at all - just reply to this newsletter. Happy Christmas. Fergus

title: Meet The Critics
author: thecrushbar
raw_article:
Hello, and welcome to The Crush Bar, a newsletter about theatre by Fergus Morgan.

This is the free Friday issue, which usually contains an interview with an exciting theatremaker or an essay on a theatre-related topic. This week, though, it features quickfire Q&As with some of the country’s leading theatre critics. After that, there are your usual three recommendations for next week: two in London, one in Scotland.

In case you missed it, here is this week’s issue of Shouts and Murmurs, which is a weekly round-up of the most interesting writing about theatre elsewhere…

You can get Shouts and Murmurs straight in your inbox every Tuesday - and help keep this newsletter going - by signing up as a paid supporter of The Crush Bar.

Usually, this costs £5/month or £50/year, but I am still offering a Black Friday Discount until midnight, so you can sign up for just £4/month or £40/year.

Theatres, companies, agencies and other organisations can also sign-up as champion supporters of The Crush Bar, which gets their name in this dedicated tab on The Crush Bar’s Substack homepage and in the footer of every Friday issue.

Get the discount here

How much do you know about theatre critics?

How many could you name? Who do they write for? What do they like? What do they not like? What shows have they championed? Who are they beyond their byline?

As regular readers know, I was at a symposium hosted by the Abbey Theatre in Dublin recently, where writers from Ireland, the UK, the USA and Canada met to discuss the current state of criticism. A report will be published at some point early next year, which should make for interesting reading, but one thing that stuck out to me was how little artists and audiences knew about the people that review them.

In popular culture, critics are often characterised as sneering elites (Anton Ego in Ratatouille) or failed creatives (Ted Wallace in The Hippopotamus). Inside the industry, they are frequently labelled privileged, bitter or vindictive, sometimes publicly. In comment sections, they are regularly abused as ignorant or out-of-touch.

In my experience, though, with few exceptions, none of that is true. In my experience, critics are knowledgeable and witty. They are curious, in both senses of the word. Above all, they are deeply, deeply passionate about theatre and want to see shows succeed. They are not bad people. They just have strong opinions and tight deadlines.

For several reasons - fostering healthier dialogue, building a richer critical conversation, and, well, sheer curiosity - I think it would be good if we got to know these much-misunderstood species better. And so I sent the country’s leading critics a quick Q&A, offering them the chance to introduce themselves. What was the first show they loved? What devices make them groan? Which artists do they admire?

This is a busy time of year, so I didn’t expect many to reply. They have, though, leaving me in the slightly awkward position of having way too much content for one newsletter. I know how fragile critics’ egos are, though, as I have one myself, so I didn’t want to leave anyone out. Welcome, then, to an extremely long newsletter, in which eleven of the country’s leading critics offer an insight into their life and work…

Dominic Cavendish

Who do you write for and where do you cover? The Daily Telegraph, and I go all over the place!

What was the first show you loved? Brian Cox in Titus Andronicus with the RSC at the Barbican in 1988.

What was the last show you loved? Jobsworth at the Park Theatre.

Which playwright/director/actor’s work are you always excited to see? Rebecca Frecknall, Rupert Goold, Ryan Calais Cameron. And I love almost all actors.

What theatrical devices make you groan? People walking in slo-mo, especially wielding umbrellas.

What do you love about your job? That I get to scamper around the world capital of theatre.

What frustrates you about your job? The lack of work-life balance and sleep.

Has a review ever caused you trouble? A satirical snipe about Stewart Lee went, um, viral.

What is a fun fact about you? I’m a Polish citizen.

Arifa Akbar

Who do you write for and where do you cover? The Guardian, across the UK, sometimes abroad.

What was the last show you loved? Alice: Return To Wonderland at the Sherman Theatre in Cardiff, which I saw last night.

Which playwright/director/actor’s work are you always excited to see? Lesley Manville, Ola Ince, Omar Elerian, Samuel Beckett, Lucy Prebble… I could go on…

What theatrical devices make you groan? Slo-mo scenes.

What do you love about your job? The speed and adrenaline, which focuses my mind. Travelling around the country when the trains are working.

What frustrates you about your job? The speed and adrenaline, when it makes me nervous about doing justice to a show in my write-up. And travelling around the country when the trains are delayed.

Has a review ever caused you trouble? Yes. Lots of noise on social media and emails from male directors who feel terribly wronged. Ignore, ignore, ignore.

What is a fun fact about you? I am very scared of the dark.

Sarah Crompton

Who do you write for and where do you cover? Theatre for WhatsOnStage, dance for The Observer, culture in general for various publications, including Vogue.

What was the first show you loved? Jonathan Pryce in Comedians by Trevor Griffiths. I was quite young when I saw it and quoted it for years.

What was the last show you loved? All My Sons in the West End. Devastatingly good.

Which playwright/director/actor’s work are you always excited to see? I am honestly nearly always excited to go to the theatre. I love too many actors to list, and feel a thrill of interest every time I see a new play. Recently, I’ve been inspired by Alice Birch, Ava Pickett, Beth Steel. I like work by women. But I also adore Roy Williams, James Graham, and David Eldridge. Directors? Rob Icke, Ian Rickson, Rebecca Frecknall, Alexander Zeldin… the list is too long…

What theatrical devices make you groan? None if used well. Everything if used badly.

What do you love about your job? It is constantly stimulating and keeps me on my toes. I learn something every time I go to the theatre and I like that. I am also, very often, hugely entertained.

What frustrates you about your job? Too much to see, too little time…

Has a review ever caused you trouble? I was the only person who didn’t adore Girl From The North Country, and I got teased about that.

What is a fun fact about you? I am as obsessed with sport as I am with the arts. I also recently took up running. Very slowly.

Clive Davis

Who do you write for and where do you cover? The Times. London and regions.

What was the first show you loved? The Playboy of the West Indies at the Tricycle.

What was the last show you loved? The Assembled Parties at the Hampstead Theatre.

Which playwright/director/actor’s work are you always excited to see? Janie Dee.

What theatrical devices make you groan? Clumsy background music. See: All My Sons in the West End.

What do you love about your job? It is a form of adult education with no student loans.

What frustrates you about your job? Online commenters who don’t read reviews properly.

Has a review ever caused you trouble? I won’t mention the title, but a playwright once accused me of being rude about the physical appearance of the actors. I was actually responding to a line in the play…

What is a fun fact about you? I spend part of each week on a narrowboat.

Andrzej Lukowski

Who do you write for and where do you cover? I’m employed by Time Out London as the theatre editor and lead critic, though I dabble here and there freelance.

What was the first show you loved? Kneehigh’s Nights at the Circus

What was the last show you loved? Ivo Van Hove’s All My Sons

Which playwright/director/actor’s work are you always excited to see? Caryl Churchill, Rob Icke, Patsy Ferran.

What theatrical devices make you groan? Accentitis: when everyone inexplicably has a different, put-on accent in a revival of a classic play. Double groan if this involves light relief characters from Birmingham or Eastern Europe.

What do you love about your job? I love that is is my job to respond to work that is essentially outside my control or that of my employer, which means it is quite hard to become institutionalised. I think a lot of people’s jobs can involve them being entirely consumed by the inner workings of the company they work for. Conversely, I think there is this image of critics as a bunch of lone wolf oddballs, and I actually love the fact that I am part of a team and a group of colleagues.

What frustrates you about your job? In all honesty, it is a very privileged job to have and my frustrations are largely to do with the precarious state of journalistic institutions and little to do with the work I see. To some extent, I feel like an anthropologist documenting an alien civilisation in an unusually sassy fashion: you don’t get frustrated with the aliens if their folk rituals aren’t to your exact taste. Please, nobody think any longer on this metaphor. It definitely cannot take it.

Has a review ever caused you trouble? What is trouble? I think because I’m male and relatively obscure then I tend not to ruffle too many feathers, and I like to think I have a quite considered way of writing that maybe shuts down accusations of being heartless. Obviously I annoy people all the time, but I imagine I get less than a hundredth of the hassle somebody like Arifa at The Guardian gets.

What is a fun fact about you? I am Time Out’s NUJ rep. Join a union, people!

Susannah Clapp

Who do you write for and where do you cover? The Observer

What was the first show you loved? As a critic, Shockheaded Peter at West Yorkshire Playhouse.

What was the last show you loved? All My Sons. Worth it for Paapa Essiedu alone.

Which playwright/director/actor’s work are you always excited to see? Simon Stone, Lynette Linton, Caryl Churchill, William Shakespeare, Ronke Adékoluéjó, Eileen Atkins, Luke Thallon…

What theatrical devices make you groan? People waving chairs above their heads.

What do you love about your job? It expands your ears, eyes, heart and brain at its best.

What frustrates you about your job? Underwritten scripts.

Has a review ever caused you trouble? I often cause my own trouble by thinking I should have done better.

What is a fun fact about you? I am fanatical about The Archers.

Sam Marlowe

Who do you write for and where do you cover? These days, The Stage, and I’m London-based.

What was the first show you loved? Sarah Kane’s Phaedra’s Love.

What was the last show you loved? The Maids at the Donmar Warehouse.

Which playwright/director/actor’s work are you always excited to see? debbie tucker green, Jamie Lloyd, Paapa Essiedu

What theatrical devices make you groan? Lazy expositional narrative delivered in direct audience address shoehorned into a play, scene changes behind a curtain or in the dark, handheld mics just for the sake of it… I could go on…

What do you love about your job? The variety, a different story every night.

What frustrates you about your job? The scarcity of artistic risk-taking in the industry.

Has a review ever caused you trouble? Often. Anyone who becomes a critic thinking it will make them popular is delusional.

What is a fun fact about you? I front an indie-rock band.

Mark Fisher

Who do you write for and where do you cover? I work freelance and review theatre primarily for The Guardian. A few years ago, I extended my patch from exclusively Scotland to incorporate a large area of England as well. I also write for The Scotsman and The List, and sometimes do workshops based on my book How To Write About Theatre.

What was the first show you loved? I got particularly excited about Willy Russell’s Blood Brothers, which I saw on a preview performance at the Liverpool Playhouse, sitting on the back row of the circle. At the time, I was thrilled by the combination of politics, comedy, tragedy and music.

What was the last show you loved? Eat The Rich by Jade Franks at the Edinburgh Fringe felt like a festival discovery and appealed to the Blood Brothers fan in me. I’m a sucker for comedy combined with class war, especially when it comes from Liverpool.

Which playwright/director/actor’s work are you always excited to see? Robert Lepage, Peter Brook, Wils Wilson, Dominic Hill, Matthew Lenton, Theresa Heskins, Arti Banerjee.

What theatrical devices make you groan? A large number of new plays finish with a single-note sound effect followed by a blackout. A large number of mediocre productions finish with a raucous song-and-dance number that tricks the audience into thinking they’ve had a good time.

What do you love about your job? I like writing and I like theatre. It’s not far off a hobby. I’ve been immensely fortunate in that. I also like observing not just the plays but the audiences and the places where they take place. I feel like a cultural anthropologists.

What frustrates you about your job? It would be nice if newspapers and theatre were more central to people’s lives.

Has a review ever caused you trouble? A long time ago, the late Kenny Ireland was on the cusp of banning me from Edinburgh’s Royal Lyceum after comments I’d made (and he’d misinterpreted) about the theatre’s free previews. Somehow, I talked him down.

What is a fun fact about you? I host a monthly podcast for pop group XTC. It is nothing to do with theatre, but at least one playwright and one critic are listeners.

Holly Williams

Who do you write for and where do you cover? I’m the editor of Exeunt, a fellow theatre Substack covering the UK and beyond, which is home to loads of different writers, and I also write for Exeunt a lot. As a freelancer, I review shows mostly across the North and Midlands, mostly for The Times and The Stage, sometimes for The Telegraph. I also write features for lots of places, like BBC Culture, The New York Times, The i Paper, etc.

What was the first show you loved? A production of The Tinderbox at Theatre Hafren in mid-Wales sometime in the early 1990s. God knows who made it, but I was very enamoured of the three giant dog puppets…

What was the last show you loved? I rewatched Dear England when it came to Sheffield recently. It is still just a beautifully constructed play and a hugely entertaining night out, even for a football ignoramus like me.

Which playwright/director/actor’s work are you always excited to see? Tim Crouch, because he is all of those things and always exciting.

What theatrical devices make you groan? Overly woke Gen Z character bingo: stroppy about climate change, obsessed with pronouns, wearing big sloppy cardigans…

What do you love about your job? Every day is slightly different. You never see the same show twice.

What frustrates you about your job? Money, money, money, or the lack thereof. And being at the mercy of British trains. I waste a lot of time battling train Wi-Fi or delays and get far too stressed about it.

Has a review ever caused you trouble? I’ve had some pretty feedback in my time, but the trouble these days is mostly the overthinking in my own head. Although there are reviews going back a few years where I wish I’d been less impressed by my own zingers and a little kinder…

What is a fun fact about you? I have synaesthesia. Ask me and I’ll tell you what colour your name is. Fergus is yellowy.

Exeunt
Inventive, in-depth, fierce and playful writing about theatre - now straight to your inbox. The new home for Exeunt Magazine.

Tim Bano

Who do you write for and where do you cover? The Financial Times, The Standard, The Stage, Time Out, the brilliant London Theatre Review podcast (like and subscribe!) and whoever else is nice enough to commission me.

What was the first show you loved? Pre-critic, it was probably Les Mis (getting paid to review it a decade later was a nice ‘I’ve made it’ moment), but the first show that made my mind explode as a critic was Mr Burns at the Almeida. An older woman (who is still a critic) stood up at the end and loudly said, ‘Give me All My Sons any day,’ and I loved it even more.

What was the last show you loved? Paddington The Musical. Cute bear.

Which playwright/director/actor’s work are you always excited to see? Sondheim, Luke Sheppard, Rebecca Frecknall, Lesley Manville.

What theatrical devices make you groan? Slo-mo. Too many chairs on stage. Unearned riffing in musicals.

What do you love about your job? Sitting in the dark and not having to talk to people. Also, trying to grapple wit ha big and complex piece of work quite quickly, and trying to form coherent thoughts and putting them into coherent words. Also, championing a piece of work that is really good is a nice feeling.

What frustrates you about your job? Pay hasn’t increased in about a decade. Press nights always start late. The number of cash-grab musicals we have to see.

What is a fun fact about you? N/A.

Nick Curtis

Who do you write for and where do you cover? The Standard. Previously The Independent, Time Out and Plays & Players magazine. London. I also do The London Theatre Review podcast with Nick Clark, Nancy Durrant and Tim Bano.

What was the first show you loved? I can still picture Frances de la Tour’s Saint Joan at the National Theatre vividly, but I probably first loved Spike Milligan in Treasure Island, the Wimbledon Theatre panto.

What was the last show you loved? All My Sons at Wyndham’s in the West End.

Which playwright/director/actor’s work are you always excited to see? Too many to name, but James Graham, Jamie Lloyd and Ronke Adékoluéjó for starters.

What theatrical devices make you groan? Drums, swirling coats and dry ice. See: The RSC, 1988-2010-ish.

What do you love about your job? Constant hope, constant stimulation, and I get to go work in beautiful, fascinating and highly functional buildings.

What frustrates you about your job? When someone else has a better turn of phrase.

Has a review ever caused you trouble? A certain blogger tried to start a social media pile-on because I “didn’t understand” musical theatre. We’ve never even spoken and he still avoids my eye at press nights.

What is a fun fact about you? Nicholas Wright once took something I’d set and gave it to Michael Gambon as a line in his play Cressida.

Three shows to see next week

Christmas Day - Almeida Theatre, Oxford, until January 8

Sam Grabiner’s Verity Bargate Award-winning play Boys On The Verge Of Tears was a huge hit at Soho Theatre last year, winning the Olivier Award for Best Production in an Affiliate Theatre. Now, Grabiner’s next play, which focuses on a Jewish family gathering over Christmas, premieres at the Almeida Theatre, in a production from James Macdonald starring Samuel Blenkin, Nigel Lindsay and more, which runs until early January. You can read my interview with Blenkin in The Stage here, my chat with Grabiner last year below, and get tickets via the button below.

Book tickets here

Revenge: After The Levoyah - Soho Theatre, until January 24

Regular readers will know that I am a big fan of Nick Cassenbaum’s riotously funny, political-satire-cum-action-thriller two-hander Revenge: After The Levoyah, which is set in 2019 and follows a pair of Jewish siblings who get embroiled in a chaotic plot to kidnap then Labour leader Jeremy Corbyn. It was a hit at the Edinburgh Fringe last year, then ran at the Yard Theatre in January. Now, it is back at the Soho Theatre. You can read my interview with Cassenbaum here and get tickets via the button below.

Book tickets here

Dancing Shoes - Traverse Theatre, until December 20

Stephen Christopher and Graeme Smith’s three-handed comedy first ran at Oran Mor as part of A Play, A Pie and A Pint’s Spring season earlier this year, where its story of recovering addicts finding solace and friendship via a viral dance video earned glowing reviews. Now, Brian Logan’s production arrives at the Traverse Theatre, where it runs until just before Christmas. You can get tickets via the button below.

Fergus
p_text: Hello, and welcome to The Crush Bar, a newsletter about theatre by Fergus Morgan. This is the free Friday issue, which usually contains an interview with an exciting theatremaker or an essay on a theatre-related topic. This week, though, it features quickfire Q&As with some of the country’s leading theatre critics. After that, there are your usual three recommendations for next week: two in London, one in Scotland. In case you missed it, here is this week’s issue of Shouts and Murmurs, which is a weekly round-up of the most interesting writing about theatre elsewhere… You can get Shouts and Murmurs straight in your inbox every Tuesday - and help keep this newsletter going - by signing up as a paid supporter of The Crush Bar. Usually, this costs £5/month or £50/year, but I am still offering a Black Friday Discount until midnight, so you can sign up for just £4/month or £40/year. Theatres, companies, agencies and other organisations can also sign-up as champion supporters of The Crush Bar, which gets their name in this dedicated tab on The Crush Bar’s Substack homepage and in the footer of every Friday issue. Get the discount here How many could you name? Who do they write for? What do they like? What do they not like? What shows have they championed? Who are they beyond their byline? As regular readers know, I was at a symposium hosted by the Abbey Theatre in Dublin recently, where writers from Ireland, the UK, the USA and Canada met to discuss the current state of criticism. A report will be published at some point early next year, which should make for interesting reading, but one thing that stuck out to me was how little artists and audiences knew about the people that review them. In popular culture, critics are often characterised as sneering elites (Anton Ego in Ratatouille) or failed creatives (Ted Wallace in The Hippopotamus). Inside the industry, they are frequently labelled privileged, bitter or vindictive, sometimes publicly. In comment sections, they are regularly abused as ignorant or out-of-touch. In my experience, though, with few exceptions, none of that is true. In my experience, critics are knowledgeable and witty. They are curious, in both senses of the word. Above all, they are deeply, deeply passionate about theatre and want to see shows succeed. They are not bad people. They just have strong opinions and tight deadlines. For several reasons - fostering healthier dialogue, building a richer critical conversation, and, well, sheer curiosity - I think it would be good if we got to know these much-misunderstood species better. And so I sent the country’s leading critics a quick Q&A, offering them the chance to introduce themselves. What was the first show they loved? What devices make them groan? Which artists do they admire? This is a busy time of year, so I didn’t expect many to reply. They have, though, leaving me in the slightly awkward position of having way too much content for one newsletter. I know how fragile critics’ egos are, though, as I have one myself, so I didn’t want to leave anyone out. Welcome, then, to an extremely long newsletter, in which eleven of the country’s leading critics offer an insight into their life and work… Who do you write for and where do you cover? The Daily Telegraph, and I go all over the place! What was the first show you loved? Brian Cox in Titus Andronicus with the RSC at the Barbican in 1988. What was the last show you loved? Jobsworth at the Park Theatre. Which playwright/director/actor’s work are you always excited to see? Rebecca Frecknall, Rupert Goold, Ryan Calais Cameron. And I love almost all actors. What theatrical devices make you groan? People walking in slo-mo, especially wielding umbrellas. What do you love about your job? That I get to scamper around the world capital of theatre. What frustrates you about your job? The lack of work-life balance and sleep. Has a review ever caused you trouble? A satirical snipe about Stewart Lee went, um, viral. What is a fun fact about you? I’m a Polish citizen. Who do you write for and where do you cover? The Guardian, across the UK, sometimes abroad. What was the last show you loved? Alice: Return To Wonderland at the Sherman Theatre in Cardiff, which I saw last night. Which playwright/director/actor’s work are you always excited to see? Lesley Manville, Ola Ince, Omar Elerian, Samuel Beckett, Lucy Prebble… I could go on… What theatrical devices make you groan? Slo-mo scenes. What do you love about your job? The speed and adrenaline, which focuses my mind. Travelling around the country when the trains are working. What frustrates you about your job? The speed and adrenaline, when it makes me nervous about doing justice to a show in my write-up. And travelling around the country when the trains are delayed. Has a review ever caused you trouble? Yes. Lots of noise on social media and emails from male directors who feel terribly wronged. Ignore, ignore, ignore. What is a fun fact about you? I am very scared of the dark. Who do you write for and where do you cover? Theatre for WhatsOnStage, dance for The Observer, culture in general for various publications, including Vogue. What was the first show you loved? Jonathan Pryce in Comedians by Trevor Griffiths. I was quite young when I saw it and quoted it for years. What was the last show you loved? All My Sons in the West End. Devastatingly good. Which playwright/director/actor’s work are you always excited to see? I am honestly nearly always excited to go to the theatre. I love too many actors to list, and feel a thrill of interest every time I see a new play. Recently, I’ve been inspired by Alice Birch, Ava Pickett, Beth Steel. I like work by women. But I also adore Roy Williams, James Graham, and David Eldridge. Directors? Rob Icke, Ian Rickson, Rebecca Frecknall, Alexander Zeldin… the list is too long… What theatrical devices make you groan? None if used well. Everything if used badly. What do you love about your job? It is constantly stimulating and keeps me on my toes. I learn something every time I go to the theatre and I like that. I am also, very often, hugely entertained. What frustrates you about your job? Too much to see, too little time… Has a review ever caused you trouble? I was the only person who didn’t adore Girl From The North Country, and I got teased about that. What is a fun fact about you? I am as obsessed with sport as I am with the arts. I also recently took up running. Very slowly. Who do you write for and where do you cover? The Times. London and regions. What was the first show you loved? The Playboy of the West Indies at the Tricycle. What was the last show you loved? The Assembled Parties at the Hampstead Theatre. Which playwright/director/actor’s work are you always excited to see? Janie Dee. What theatrical devices make you groan? Clumsy background music. See: All My Sons in the West End. What do you love about your job? It is a form of adult education with no student loans. What frustrates you about your job? Online commenters who don’t read reviews properly. Has a review ever caused you trouble? I won’t mention the title, but a playwright once accused me of being rude about the physical appearance of the actors. I was actually responding to a line in the play… What is a fun fact about you? I spend part of each week on a narrowboat. Who do you write for and where do you cover? I’m employed by Time Out London as the theatre editor and lead critic, though I dabble here and there freelance. What was the first show you loved? Kneehigh’s Nights at the Circus What was the last show you loved? Ivo Van Hove’s All My Sons Which playwright/director/actor’s work are you always excited to see? Caryl Churchill, Rob Icke, Patsy Ferran. What theatrical devices make you groan? Accentitis: when everyone inexplicably has a different, put-on accent in a revival of a classic play. Double groan if this involves light relief characters from Birmingham or Eastern Europe. What do you love about your job? I love that is is my job to respond to work that is essentially outside my control or that of my employer, which means it is quite hard to become institutionalised. I think a lot of people’s jobs can involve them being entirely consumed by the inner workings of the company they work for. Conversely, I think there is this image of critics as a bunch of lone wolf oddballs, and I actually love the fact that I am part of a team and a group of colleagues. What frustrates you about your job? In all honesty, it is a very privileged job to have and my frustrations are largely to do with the precarious state of journalistic institutions and little to do with the work I see. To some extent, I feel like an anthropologist documenting an alien civilisation in an unusually sassy fashion: you don’t get frustrated with the aliens if their folk rituals aren’t to your exact taste. Please, nobody think any longer on this metaphor. It definitely cannot take it. Has a review ever caused you trouble? What is trouble? I think because I’m male and relatively obscure then I tend not to ruffle too many feathers, and I like to think I have a quite considered way of writing that maybe shuts down accusations of being heartless. Obviously I annoy people all the time, but I imagine I get less than a hundredth of the hassle somebody like Arifa at The Guardian gets. What is a fun fact about you? I am Time Out’s NUJ rep. Join a union, people! Who do you write for and where do you cover? The Observer What was the first show you loved? As a critic, Shockheaded Peter at West Yorkshire Playhouse. What was the last show you loved? All My Sons. Worth it for Paapa Essiedu alone. Which playwright/director/actor’s work are you always excited to see? Simon Stone, Lynette Linton, Caryl Churchill, William Shakespeare, Ronke Adékoluéjó, Eileen Atkins, Luke Thallon… What theatrical devices make you groan? People waving chairs above their heads. What do you love about your job? It expands your ears, eyes, heart and brain at its best. What frustrates you about your job? Underwritten scripts. Has a review ever caused you trouble? I often cause my own trouble by thinking I should have done better. What is a fun fact about you? I am fanatical about The Archers. Who do you write for and where do you cover? These days, The Stage, and I’m London-based. What was the first show you loved? Sarah Kane’s Phaedra’s Love. What was the last show you loved? The Maids at the Donmar Warehouse. Which playwright/director/actor’s work are you always excited to see? debbie tucker green, Jamie Lloyd, Paapa Essiedu What theatrical devices make you groan? Lazy expositional narrative delivered in direct audience address shoehorned into a play, scene changes behind a curtain or in the dark, handheld mics just for the sake of it… I could go on… What do you love about your job? The variety, a different story every night. What frustrates you about your job? The scarcity of artistic risk-taking in the industry. Has a review ever caused you trouble? Often. Anyone who becomes a critic thinking it will make them popular is delusional. What is a fun fact about you? I front an indie-rock band. Who do you write for and where do you cover? I work freelance and review theatre primarily for The Guardian. A few years ago, I extended my patch from exclusively Scotland to incorporate a large area of England as well. I also write for The Scotsman and The List, and sometimes do workshops based on my book How To Write About Theatre. What was the first show you loved? I got particularly excited about Willy Russell’s Blood Brothers, which I saw on a preview performance at the Liverpool Playhouse, sitting on the back row of the circle. At the time, I was thrilled by the combination of politics, comedy, tragedy and music. What was the last show you loved? Eat The Rich by Jade Franks at the Edinburgh Fringe felt like a festival discovery and appealed to the Blood Brothers fan in me. I’m a sucker for comedy combined with class war, especially when it comes from Liverpool. Which playwright/director/actor’s work are you always excited to see? Robert Lepage, Peter Brook, Wils Wilson, Dominic Hill, Matthew Lenton, Theresa Heskins, Arti Banerjee. What theatrical devices make you groan? A large number of new plays finish with a single-note sound effect followed by a blackout. A large number of mediocre productions finish with a raucous song-and-dance number that tricks the audience into thinking they’ve had a good time. What do you love about your job? I like writing and I like theatre. It’s not far off a hobby. I’ve been immensely fortunate in that. I also like observing not just the plays but the audiences and the places where they take place. I feel like a cultural anthropologists. What frustrates you about your job? It would be nice if newspapers and theatre were more central to people’s lives. Has a review ever caused you trouble? A long time ago, the late Kenny Ireland was on the cusp of banning me from Edinburgh’s Royal Lyceum after comments I’d made (and he’d misinterpreted) about the theatre’s free previews. Somehow, I talked him down. What is a fun fact about you? I host a monthly podcast for pop group XTC. It is nothing to do with theatre, but at least one playwright and one critic are listeners. Who do you write for and where do you cover? I’m the editor of Exeunt, a fellow theatre Substack covering the UK and beyond, which is home to loads of different writers, and I also write for Exeunt a lot. As a freelancer, I review shows mostly across the North and Midlands, mostly for The Times and The Stage, sometimes for The Telegraph. I also write features for lots of places, like BBC Culture, The New York Times, The i Paper, etc. What was the first show you loved? A production of The Tinderbox at Theatre Hafren in mid-Wales sometime in the early 1990s. God knows who made it, but I was very enamoured of the three giant dog puppets… What was the last show you loved? I rewatched Dear England when it came to Sheffield recently. It is still just a beautifully constructed play and a hugely entertaining night out, even for a football ignoramus like me. Which playwright/director/actor’s work are you always excited to see? Tim Crouch, because he is all of those things and always exciting. What theatrical devices make you groan? Overly woke Gen Z character bingo: stroppy about climate change, obsessed with pronouns, wearing big sloppy cardigans… What do you love about your job? Every day is slightly different. You never see the same show twice. What frustrates you about your job? Money, money, money, or the lack thereof. And being at the mercy of British trains. I waste a lot of time battling train Wi-Fi or delays and get far too stressed about it. Has a review ever caused you trouble? I’ve had some pretty feedback in my time, but the trouble these days is mostly the overthinking in my own head. Although there are reviews going back a few years where I wish I’d been less impressed by my own zingers and a little kinder… What is a fun fact about you? I have synaesthesia. Ask me and I’ll tell you what colour your name is. Fergus is yellowy. Who do you write for and where do you cover? The Financial Times, The Standard, The Stage, Time Out, the brilliant London Theatre Review podcast (like and subscribe!) and whoever else is nice enough to commission me. What was the first show you loved? Pre-critic, it was probably Les Mis (getting paid to review it a decade later was a nice ‘I’ve made it’ moment), but the first show that made my mind explode as a critic was Mr Burns at the Almeida. An older woman (who is still a critic) stood up at the end and loudly said, ‘Give me All My Sons any day,’ and I loved it even more. What was the last show you loved? Paddington The Musical. Cute bear. Which playwright/director/actor’s work are you always excited to see? Sondheim, Luke Sheppard, Rebecca Frecknall, Lesley Manville. What theatrical devices make you groan? Slo-mo. Too many chairs on stage. Unearned riffing in musicals. What do you love about your job? Sitting in the dark and not having to talk to people. Also, trying to grapple wit ha big and complex piece of work quite quickly, and trying to form coherent thoughts and putting them into coherent words. Also, championing a piece of work that is really good is a nice feeling. What frustrates you about your job? Pay hasn’t increased in about a decade. Press nights always start late. The number of cash-grab musicals we have to see. What is a fun fact about you? N/A. Who do you write for and where do you cover? The Standard. Previously The Independent, Time Out and Plays & Players magazine. London. I also do The London Theatre Review podcast with Nick Clark, Nancy Durrant and Tim Bano. What was the first show you loved? I can still picture Frances de la Tour’s Saint Joan at the National Theatre vividly, but I probably first loved Spike Milligan in Treasure Island, the Wimbledon Theatre panto. What was the last show you loved? All My Sons at Wyndham’s in the West End. Which playwright/director/actor’s work are you always excited to see? Too many to name, but James Graham, Jamie Lloyd and Ronke Adékoluéjó for starters. What theatrical devices make you groan? Drums, swirling coats and dry ice. See: The RSC, 1988-2010-ish. What do you love about your job? Constant hope, constant stimulation, and I get to go work in beautiful, fascinating and highly functional buildings. What frustrates you about your job? When someone else has a better turn of phrase. Has a review ever caused you trouble? A certain blogger tried to start a social media pile-on because I “didn’t understand” musical theatre. We’ve never even spoken and he still avoids my eye at press nights. What is a fun fact about you? Nicholas Wright once took something I’d set and gave it to Michael Gambon as a line in his play Cressida. title: "We wanted to see if true crime could work on stage"
author: thecrushbar
raw_article:
Hello, and welcome to The Crush Bar, a newsletter about theatre by Fergus Morgan.

This is the free Friday issue, which usually contains an interview with an exciting theatremaker or an essay on a theatre-related topic. This week, it is a chat with actor, writer and producer Jack Holden, who has several different projects on the go, including the return of acclaimed one-man play Kenrex at The Other Palace. After that, there are your usual three show recommendations for next week.

In case you missed it, here is this week’s issue of Shouts and Murmurs, which is a weekly round-up of the most interesting writing about theatre elsewhere…

You can get Shouts and Murmurs straight in your inbox every Tuesday - and help keep this newsletter going - by signing up as a paid supporter of The Crush Bar.

Usually, this costs £5/month or £50/year, but, thrillingly, I am currently running a Black Friday Discount, meaning you can sign up for just £4/month or £40/year.

Theatres, companies, agencies and other organisations can also sign-up as champion supporters of The Crush Bar, which gets their name in this dedicated tab on The Crush Bar’s Substack homepage and in the footer of every Friday issue.

If you enjoy reading this newsletter, or a show you are involved with has featured in it, or your organisation has benefited from it somehow, then please do consider helping to keep it going by becoming a paid supporter. You can do that via the button below.

Get the discount here

It feels like Jack Holden is everywhere right now.

The versatile actor, writer and producer’s deft dramatisation of Alan Hollinghurst’s 2004 Booker Prize-winning novel The Line Of Beauty finishes its run at the Almeida Theatre this weekend: Sarah Hemming called Michael Grandage’s production a “brilliantly subtle portrait of Thatcher’s Britain” in The Financial Times. His club-night gig-theatre Peter Pan experience Club NVLRND was a huge hit among younger festival-goers at this year’s Edinburgh Festival Fringe, and is currently in search of a London home. And his acclaimed one-man play Kenrex, co-written with director Ed Stambollouian and featuring a live score from musician John Patrick Elliott, is about to start a second London run at The Other Palace, after premiering in Sheffield last November, then triumphantly transferring to Southwark Playhouse in February.

Born in 1990, Holden grew up in Kent, then trained at Bristol Old Vic Theatre School. Shortly after graduating, he landed the lead role of Albert in the National Theatre’s famous production of War Horse at the age of 21. His subsequent acting credits are impressive - Stambollouian’s absurd Fringe hit Awkward Conversations With Animals I’ve F*cked, Tom Morton-Smith’s Oppenheimer with the Royal Shakespeare Company, James Graham’s Ink at the Almeida - but Holden’s ambitions always extended further. His debut one-man play, Cruise, a gay odyssey set in 1980s Soho inspired by a call he received while volunteering for LGBTQ+ listening service Switchboard, premiered online, then did two West End runs, earning a Best New Play nod at the 2022 Olivier Awards. He has dabbled in producing since for stage and screen since 2017, too, and wrote and directed his first short film, Safari, earlier this year. Like I said, everywhere.

Kenrex is another one-man play, like your debut Cruise. What is it about?

It is based on a true story that took place in the town of Skidmore, Missouri, between in the late 1970s and early 1980s. The town was basically terrorised by a guy called Ken Rex McElroy for years. He was a bully. He stole stuff and threated people. He was a petty criminal but, although he was tried for 21 crimes, he never spent a night in jail because he had this amazing defence attorney. That ultimately led to the townsfolk deciding enough was enough and taking matters into their own hands. The story is kind of an indictment of the American justice system, which basically leaves these pockets of the Midwest to their own devices.

The guy’s name was Ken Rex McElroy, but your show is called Kenrex.

We went around the houses to find a name. We landed on Kenrex because it just sticks in people’s heads. It becomes this mythic thing, like the name of a monster. And it is really searchable. When people Google it, it’s like, bam, there is the show.

Where did the idea come from?

Ed Stambollouian, the co-writer and director, and I wanted to see if true crime could work on stage. We’ve been developing this show for a long time so this was a while ago. It was around the time of S-Town and Serial and Making A Murderer were big. There were a few stories on our shortlist. This one stuck because of its setting in small town America, which is perfect for the genre, because it is unsolved, and because there is so much detail out there. The story is so rich and it builds to this huge theatrical moment. That presented itself as the ultimate theatrical challenge.

What other stories were on the shortlist?

There was a couple of gruesome British crimes on there. There were a couple more in America, too. There was this spate of plane-jackings in America in the 1970s, and there was this terrifying story about a guy who went into drug stores in the USA and replaced pills with poison, causing this huge hysteria across the country. Ultimately we landed on Kenrex, though, because there was so much coverage of it. It has taken on this kind of mythic quality is a story, too. It has become a fable and that seemed to legitimise us telling the story as British theatremakers.

Was it always going to be a one-man play?

It was originally imagined with me doing all the acting and John Patrick Elliot playing all the music. There was a phase in the process where we were invited to explore turning it into a full-scale play with a cast of ten and a band of four, which would have been cool, but ultimately we came back to the original idea. There is something about watching me sweat and spin all the plates that is part of the enjoyment. There is also something about that form that works, too. In a true crime podcast, you hear everything in sequence. You hear a bit of narration, then a court transcript, then an interview, one voice at a time. On stage, that actually could be one voice, with different accents and effects. That’s the trick we play.

Are all the characters based on real people?

There is only one character that is an amalgamation to strengthen the narrative. Otherwise they are all completely real. People have a fun time Googling them after and comparing my theatrical interpretations with the real people. Sometimes they are not the same, but I have to turn up the heat on them a bit for clarity.

This is your second one-man play, after Cruise. Do you enjoy that form?

I guess I do. I really believe in the form. It is an incredibly lean way of telling a story. There is something I like about drawing the audience’s eyes to a singular focus, about tracking a single point throughout an entire show, and there is something about asking the audience to do a lot of the imaginative work themselves, too. It is a highly technical show, but it is also very stripped back and simple. It is pure theatre. That is the kind of theatre I believe in.

Kenrex was a hit in Sheffield. This is its second London run. Was that the plan?

We always planned for it to go to Southwark Playhouse. That was the only way it made financial sense for Katy [Lipson], our producer, and even then it didn’t really make sense. We knew there was more life in it, though. The word-of-mouth was insane and there was a huge list of people signed up for return tickets, so we thought: ‘Okay, it has to come back.’ It was just a puzzle of finding the right venue.

Were you a theatre kid growing up?

I was. I went to quite an academic school and there wasn’t much of a drama offering, but I was always putting on plays and concerts and stuff. It was never really about fronting the show or being the star. It was always about just making stuff. It was about staying after school and painting the flats and finding the costumes and bringing it all together. That is where the magic is for me.

You trained at Bristol Old Vic Theatre School, then landed the lead role in War Horse, then worked as an actor for the following eight years. You were in some fantastic shows - Awkward Conversations With Animals I’ve F*cked, Oppenheimer, Ink - but did you feel there was a side to yourself you were not exploring?

I did, and very early on. I did the Royal Court Writers Group at the beginning of 2014, just a few years after I’d left drama school. I was already getting itchy feet. Also, I’d just finished War Horse and was faced with the reality of being out of work and feeling like nothing was within my power. Since then, I’ve written stuff and produced stuff and acted in stuff. I’m getting more and more back into that holistic way of operating as an all-round theatremaker. That is definitely the space I want to occupy. There were many years when there would be six months between jobs. I found that so frustrating. Having a lot more agency is really rewarding.

You seem to be doing so much now: Kenrex, The Line Of Beauty, Club NVRLND. Where do you find the energy?

I find energy and momentum in the projects and I feel most drained when things aren’t happening. It is kind of a vicious cycle. The more things roll on, the more I want to keep feeding the fire. I definitely say yes to too much stuff, and end up having to work all day then write all evening and at the weekend, but most successful writers I know, like James Graham and Russell T Davies, do that as well.

The Line Of Beauty is a brilliantly elegant adaptation of Alan Hollinghurst’s novel about class and wealth and politics and the AIDS crisis. How did it come about?

I talking with a friend about how much we love the book. We wondered why it hadn’t been done on stage. I sent an email to Alan’s agent asking if the rights were available. They were, so I approached the Almeida and asked them if they were interested. They said yes. Then I was like: ‘Oh God, now I actually have to do it.’

It is the first thing you have had staged that is not a one-man play, too.

It is. It was a huge step up from Cruise and Kenrex. Adaptation of such an enormous book is more like curation, though. Actually, with a 500-page book like The Line Of Beauty, it is more like subtraction. You cut and cut and refine and refine. Quite early on, I had to make piece with the fact that it would disappoint some fans of the book because it was just impossible to fit everything in. You have to turn it into something that is compelling to people that don’t know the book at all, though.

What was your relationship with Alan like? Was he helpful? Did he leave you to it?

He was helpful in that he left me to it. By his own admission, his instinct would have been to come back with novelistic notes, to ask why a scene had been conflated into a line. Ultimately, he knew that is what you have to do if you don’t want a nine-hour night at the theatre, though, so he was very wise and magnanimous. He came back with some precise, acute notes about the conflation of characters. I had conflated nine characters into one and they had become a bit of a Frankenstein. He was very gracious about it all, though, and it was lovely watching him watch the final rehearsal room runs and be moved by theatrical versions of his characters. Working with him and Michael at the Almeida was very intimidating. I had huge imposter syndrome. But it was hugely rewarding, too.

Having it staged by Michael Grandage at the Almeida Theatre must is amazing, too. He is such a classy director and it is such a powerful space.

Totally. And the audience at the Almeida are incredibly theatre literate, too.

Tell me about your Peter Pan-themed gig-theatre club-night musical Club NVRLND.

I was approached by the producer and director for that. They had seen Cruise and were wondering who could write a camp musical adaptation of Peter Pan set in a club and for some reason they thought of me. I am quite jaded and cynical, so my instinct was to set it in a club that was quite seedy and bleak, but that had this heart and joy underneath. It shares some DNA with Cruise in that.

It was a huge hit at the Edinburgh Fringe this year. What was that like?

It was a blisteringly terrifying experience throwing it up in Edinburgh without the time to refine it, and within the painful limitations of the festival. It took off quite quickly, though. Originally we were going to call it Neverland, but the Great Ormond Street Hospital, who look after the rights for Peter Pan, said we couldn’t do that, so we changed it to Club NVRLND, and that was actually a masterstroke because it told the audience exactly what it was going to be. A few people came expecting a trad show and were like: ‘Oh God, what the hell is happening?’ Once word spread, though, people knew what to expect. A lot of people said they wished Club NVRLND was what going to a club was really like. Some people find clubs horrible, intrusive places, whereas Club NVRLND is very safe and sugary fun.

How many times did you go?

I went seven times across the whole first week. I was quite tired after that.

What is the plan for now?

The plan is to find a bespoke club venue for it that we can take over, a bit like Oscar at the crown, somewhere we can get the sound and lights on point, and that we don’t have only 20 minutes to get in every night, which was just hell.

And what are you up to next?

I’m really proud of Kenrex. I think it is going to have a long life. And next year is looking really busy, too. Some stuff will be announced in the coming weeks.

Kenrex is at The Other Palace until February 1. The Line Of Beauty is at the Almeida Theatre until November 29.

This is the free Friday issue, which usually contains an interview with an exciting theatremaker or an essay on a theatre-related topic. This week, it is a chat with actor, writer and producer Jack Holden, who has several different projects on the go, including the return of acclaimed one-man play Kenrex at The Other Palace. After that, there are your usual three show recommendations for next week. In case you missed it, here is this week’s issue of Shouts and Murmurs, which is a weekly round-up of the most interesting writing about theatre elsewhere… You can get Shouts and Murmurs straight in your inbox every Tuesday - and help keep this newsletter going - by signing up as a paid supporter of The Crush Bar. If you enjoy reading this newsletter, or a show you are involved with has featured in it, or your organisation has benefited from it somehow, then please do consider helping to keep it going by becoming a paid supporter. You can do that via the button below. Get the discount here The versatile actor, writer and producer’s deft dramatisation of Alan Hollinghurst’s 2004 Booker Prize-winning novel The Line Of Beauty finishes its run at the Almeida Theatre this weekend: Sarah Hemming called Michael Grandage’s production a “brilliantly subtle portrait of Thatcher’s Britain” in The Financial Times. His club-night gig-theatre Peter Pan experience Club NVLRND was a huge hit among younger festival-goers at this year’s Edinburgh Festival Fringe, and is currently in search of a London home. And his acclaimed one-man play Kenrex, co-written with director Ed Stambollouian and featuring a live score from musician John Patrick Elliott, is about to start a second London run at The Other Palace, after premiering in Sheffield last November, then triumphantly transferring to Southwark Playhouse in February. Born in 1990, Holden grew up in Kent, then trained at Bristol Old Vic Theatre School. Shortly after graduating, he landed the lead role of Albert in the National Theatre’s famous production of War Horse at the age of 21. His subsequent acting credits are impressive - Stambollouian’s absurd Fringe hit Awkward Conversations With Animals I’ve F*cked, Tom Morton-Smith’s Oppenheimer with the Royal Shakespeare Company, James Graham’s Ink at the Almeida - but Holden’s ambitions always extended further. His debut one-man play, Cruise, a gay odyssey set in 1980s Soho inspired by a call he received while volunteering for LGBTQ+ listening service Switchboard, premiered online, then did two West End runs, earning a Best New Play nod at the 2022 Olivier Awards. He has dabbled in producing since for stage and screen since 2017, too, and wrote and directed his first short film, Safari, earlier this year. Like I said, everywhere. Kenrex is another one-man play, like your debut Cruise. What is it about? It is based on a true story that took place in the town of Skidmore, Missouri, between in the late 1970s and early 1980s. The town was basically terrorised by a guy called Ken Rex McElroy for years. He was a bully. He stole stuff and threated people. He was a petty criminal but, although he was tried for 21 crimes, he never spent a night in jail because he had this amazing defence attorney. That ultimately led to the townsfolk deciding enough was enough and taking matters into their own hands. The story is kind of an indictment of the American justice system, which basically leaves these pockets of the Midwest to their own devices. The guy’s name was Ken Rex McElroy, but your show is called Kenrex. We went around the houses to find a name. We landed on Kenrex because it just sticks in people’s heads. It becomes this mythic thing, like the name of a monster. And it is really searchable. When people Google it, it’s like, bam, there is the show. Where did the idea come from? Ed Stambollouian, the co-writer and director, and I wanted to see if true crime could work on stage. We’ve been developing this show for a long time so this was a while ago. It was around the time of S-Town and Serial and Making A Murderer were big. There were a few stories on our shortlist. This one stuck because of its setting in small town America, which is perfect for the genre, because it is unsolved, and because there is so much detail out there. The story is so rich and it builds to this huge theatrical moment. That presented itself as the ultimate theatrical challenge. What other stories were on the shortlist? There was a couple of gruesome British crimes on there. There were a couple more in America, too. There was this spate of plane-jackings in America in the 1970s, and there was this terrifying story about a guy who went into drug stores in the USA and replaced pills with poison, causing this huge hysteria across the country. Ultimately we landed on Kenrex, though, because there was so much coverage of it. It has taken on this kind of mythic quality is a story, too. It has become a fable and that seemed to legitimise us telling the story as British theatremakers. Was it always going to be a one-man play? It was originally imagined with me doing all the acting and John Patrick Elliot playing all the music. There was a phase in the process where we were invited to explore turning it into a full-scale play with a cast of ten and a band of four, which would have been cool, but ultimately we came back to the original idea. There is something about watching me sweat and spin all the plates that is part of the enjoyment. There is also something about that form that works, too. In a true crime podcast, you hear everything in sequence. You hear a bit of narration, then a court transcript, then an interview, one voice at a time. On stage, that actually could be one voice, with different accents and effects. That’s the trick we play. Are all the characters based on real people? There is only one character that is an amalgamation to strengthen the narrative. Otherwise they are all completely real. People have a fun time Googling them after and comparing my theatrical interpretations with the real people. Sometimes they are not the same, but I have to turn up the heat on them a bit for clarity. This is your second one-man play, after Cruise. Do you enjoy that form? I guess I do. I really believe in the form. It is an incredibly lean way of telling a story. There is something I like about drawing the audience’s eyes to a singular focus, about tracking a single point throughout an entire show, and there is something about asking the audience to do a lot of the imaginative work themselves, too. It is a highly technical show, but it is also very stripped back and simple. It is pure theatre. That is the kind of theatre I believe in. Kenrex was a hit in Sheffield. This is its second London run. Was that the plan? We always planned for it to go to Southwark Playhouse. That was the only way it made financial sense for Katy [Lipson], our producer, and even then it didn’t really make sense. We knew there was more life in it, though. The word-of-mouth was insane and there was a huge list of people signed up for return tickets, so we thought: ‘Okay, it has to come back.’ It was just a puzzle of finding the right venue. Were you a theatre kid growing up? I was. I went to quite an academic school and there wasn’t much of a drama offering, but I was always putting on plays and concerts and stuff. It was never really about fronting the show or being the star. It was always about just making stuff. It was about staying after school and painting the flats and finding the costumes and bringing it all together. That is where the magic is for me. You trained at Bristol Old Vic Theatre School, then landed the lead role in War Horse, then worked as an actor for the following eight years. You were in some fantastic shows - Awkward Conversations With Animals I’ve F*cked, Oppenheimer, Ink - but did you feel there was a side to yourself you were not exploring? I did, and very early on. I did the Royal Court Writers Group at the beginning of 2014, just a few years after I’d left drama school. I was already getting itchy feet. Also, I’d just finished War Horse and was faced with the reality of being out of work and feeling like nothing was within my power. Since then, I’ve written stuff and produced stuff and acted in stuff. I’m getting more and more back into that holistic way of operating as an all-round theatremaker. That is definitely the space I want to occupy. There were many years when there would be six months between jobs. I found that so frustrating. Having a lot more agency is really rewarding. You seem to be doing so much now: Kenrex, The Line Of Beauty, Club NVRLND. Where do you find the energy? I find energy and momentum in the projects and I feel most drained when things aren’t happening. It is kind of a vicious cycle. The more things roll on, the more I want to keep feeding the fire. I definitely say yes to too much stuff, and end up having to work all day then write all evening and at the weekend, but most successful writers I know, like James Graham and Russell T Davies, do that as well. The Line Of Beauty is a brilliantly elegant adaptation of Alan Hollinghurst’s novel about class and wealth and politics and the AIDS crisis. How did it come about? I talking with a friend about how much we love the book. We wondered why it hadn’t been done on stage. I sent an email to Alan’s agent asking if the rights were available. They were, so I approached the Almeida and asked them if they were interested. They said yes. Then I was like: ‘Oh God, now I actually have to do it.’ It is the first thing you have had staged that is not a one-man play, too. It is. It was a huge step up from Cruise and Kenrex. Adaptation of such an enormous book is more like curation, though. Actually, with a 500-page book like The Line Of Beauty, it is more like subtraction. You cut and cut and refine and refine. Quite early on, I had to make piece with the fact that it would disappoint some fans of the book because it was just impossible to fit everything in. You have to turn it into something that is compelling to people that don’t know the book at all, though. What was your relationship with Alan like? Was he helpful? Did he leave you to it? He was helpful in that he left me to it. By his own admission, his instinct would have been to come back with novelistic notes, to ask why a scene had been conflated into a line. Ultimately, he knew that is what you have to do if you don’t want a nine-hour night at the theatre, though, so he was very wise and magnanimous. He came back with some precise, acute notes about the conflation of characters. I had conflated nine characters into one and they had become a bit of a Frankenstein. He was very gracious about it all, though, and it was lovely watching him watch the final rehearsal room runs and be moved by theatrical versions of his characters. Working with him and Michael at the Almeida was very intimidating. I had huge imposter syndrome. But it was hugely rewarding, too. Having it staged by Michael Grandage at the Almeida Theatre must is amazing, too. He is such a classy director and it is such a powerful space. Totally. And the audience at the Almeida are incredibly theatre literate, too. Tell me about your Peter Pan-themed gig-theatre club-night musical Club NVRLND. I was approached by the producer and director for that. They had seen Cruise and were wondering who could write a camp musical adaptation of Peter Pan set in a club and for some reason they thought of me. I am quite jaded and cynical, so my instinct was to set it in a club that was quite seedy and bleak, but that had this heart and joy underneath. It shares some DNA with Cruise in that. It was a huge hit at the Edinburgh Fringe this year. What was that like? It was a blisteringly terrifying experience throwing it up in Edinburgh without the time to refine it, and within the painful limitations of the festival. It took off quite quickly, though. Originally we were going to call it Neverland, but the Great Ormond Street Hospital, who look after the rights for Peter Pan, said we couldn’t do that, so we changed it to Club NVRLND, and that was actually a masterstroke because it told the audience exactly what it was going to be. A few people came expecting a trad show and were like: ‘Oh God, what the hell is happening?’ Once word spread, though, people knew what to expect. A lot of people said they wished Club NVRLND was what going to a club was really like. Some people find clubs horrible, intrusive places, whereas Club NVRLND is very safe and sugary fun. How many times did you go? I went seven times across the whole first week. I was quite tired after that. What is the plan for now? The plan is to find a bespoke club venue for it that we can take over, a bit like Oscar at the crown, somewhere we can get the sound and lights on point, and that we don’t have only 20 minutes to get in every night, which was just hell. And what are you up to next? I’m really proud of Kenrex. I think it is going to have a long life. And next year is looking really busy, too. Some stuff will be announced in the coming weeks. Kenrex is at The Other Palace until February 1. The Line Of Beauty is at the Almeida Theatre until November 29.
