title: "When I arrived here, I was surprised by how traditional productions of Shakespeare were"
author: thecrushbar
raw_article:
Hello, and welcome to The Crush Bar, a newsletter about theatre by Fergus Morgan.

This is the free Friday issue, which usually contains an interview with an exciting theatremaker or an essay on a theatre-related topic. This week, it is a chat with director Júlia Levai, whose two-man reimagining of Julius Caesar opens at the Arcola Theatre next week. After that, there are your usual three show recommendations.

In case you missed it, here is this week’s issue of Shouts and Murmurs, which is a weekly round-up of the most interesting writing about theatre elsewhere…

You can get Shouts and Murmurs straight in your inbox every Tuesday - and help keep this newsletter going - by signing up as a paid supporter of The Crush Bar.

Theatres, companies, agencies and other organisations can also sign-up as champion supporters of The Crush Bar, which gets their name in this dedicated tab on The Crush Bar’s Substack homepage and in the footer of every Friday issue.

Subscribe now

The trailblazing Buzz Goodbody had a huge impact during her tragically short career.

As the first female director at the Royal Shakespeare Company, fired by an energetic creativity and committed Marxism, she toured bold shows around schools, staged radical productions at the company’s Stratford-upon-Avon home, and, most famously, was the driving force behind The Other Place, the RSC’s experimental third space. which opened in an old tin shed rehearsal room in 1973. Goodbody committed suicide less than two years later at the shockingly young age of 28. Now, a company, Buzz Studios, has been launched in her memory by her nephew, the actor Adam Goodbody. Its first production, a two-man reimagining of Julius Caesar at the Arcola Theatre called Petty Men, is being staged by another young director inspired by her legacy.

Born and raised in Budapest, Hungary, Júlia Levai moved to Britain in 2015 to study at the University of York, then completed an MA in directing at LAMDA, graduating in 2019. Since then, she has worked as an assistant and associate director on shows at the Young Vic, the RSC, the National Theatre and elsewhere, and staged two acclaimed productions at Southwark Playhouse: Kim Davies’ Smoke, a riff on August Strindberg’s Miss Julie, in 2023 and Nancy Netherwood’s Radiant Boy: A Haunting earlier this year.

Petty Men is billed as a “radical reworking” of Julius Caesar. What is it about?

It is about two actors who are understudying the characters of Brutus and Cassius in an unnamed West End production of Shakespeare’s Julius Caesar. On the hundredth performance of the show, they decide to stage their own production of the play in their dressing room to tackle their boredom. As they do it, the production starts to take over and their friendship comes to a crisis point. Can they hold on to their own reality? Or do they get lost in the world of the play?

You have devised the show with Adam Goodbody and John Chisham, who play Cassius and Brutus. Where did the idea come from?

John and Adam brought the idea to me. At first, it was just: ‘Can we cut Julius Caesar down to two actors? Could we perform all of it through Brutus and Cassius?’ As we discussed it, quite organically, this idea of paralleling the world of theatre and the world of Rome came up, and it felt quite ripe and exciting. In the play, for example, there are two actors who are stuck in a basement while someone else gets to be the star, just as Brutus and Cassius question why Caesar has so much power in the play. And in the play, Brutus and Cassius supposedly put Rome before everything else, and there is a similar notion when it comes to theatre, too. I was interested in how far you might go to hold on to something like that.

How did you go about creating it?

First, we did an edit of Julius Caesar. Then, we created a structure for the show. Then, the three of us wrote a draft together, with each of us looking at different scenes. Then, we came together to develop it further. It is a piece of devised theatre, so the biggest revelations have always happened in the room. The draft we started with has drastically changed in the last week and a half. It has been a really enjoyable process of working and reworking it together as a collective.

What can audiences actually expect from the show?

It starts very realistically in a functioning dressing room, with a kettle, a table, a mirror, some yoga mats and all the usual bits. As it goes on, the space becomes stranger and more theatrical. It actually feels a bit closer to the kind of Shakespeare I grew up with in Hungary. In Europe, because we are working in translation, Shakespeare often becomes the starting point for creating new work. When you have to translate Shakespeare, you get much more freedom.

Do you think we are too reverential with Shakespeare in the UK?

I think so. When I arrived here, I was surprised by how traditional productions of Shakespeare were. I felt quite alienated by the attention to the language, and the history and tradition that surrounds that. I am more interested in how Shakespeare can be played around with. I think it is so much more thrilling to be in conversation with Shakespeare, rather than just doing it straight, which can be really alienating unless you really nail it. There are loads of brilliant productions that do that, though. I thought Emily Burns’ Measure For Measure was brilliant. She managed to find such a thriller in such a knotty play. I really wanted to see Hamlet Hail To The Thief, too. The most influential Shakespeare productions I’ve seen, though, are the ones that have come to the Barbican. I saw Thomas Ostermeier’s Richard III at the Barbican a while ago. That has really stuck with me.

This is the first production from a new company, Buzz Studios. Tell me about that.

It is Adam’s company. He got John and me on board for his first production. He set it up in the name of his aunt Buzz Goodbody, who, as I’m sure you know, was a pioneering female director in the 1970s. She was the first female director at the RSC and made these incredibly radical shows. She did Shakespeare in modern dress, for example. That seems obvious now, but at the time it was mind-blowing. She was passionate about making theatre accessible for young people, too. Adam has set up this company to continue her legacy. It feels really special to be part of its first production. We went to the RSC archives and went through the three boxes they have of her notes and scripts. That was really moving, especially for Adam.

What was growing up in Budapest like? How did you get into theatre?

I feel lucky to have grown up there when I did. When I was a teenager, there was a really vibrant theatre culture, similar to the rep system they have in Germany. There was so much good theatre going on and you could see it all essentially for free as a student. I worked as an usher at a theatre that is kind of our equivalent of the Almeida Theatre, and I saw a production of Hamlet there over and over and over again. I also had a really great teacher who was brilliant at making us feel curious and excited about theatre. She helped super shy people like me, who didn’t want to go on stage, discover that we could be directors or dramaturgs.

You moved here to train, studying at the University of York, then LAMDA. Why?

There were several reasons. Partly, it was because Hungary has become quite complicated politically. There is a real culture war going on against anything that encourages critical thinking, so the theatre scene has been squeezed and squeezed. I felt like if I wanted to do theatre, it was a good option to do it elsewhere.

Hungary has become increasingly authoritarian under Viktor Orbán. How has that affected the country’s theatre scene?

There is a lot of resistance and a lot of brilliant artists still making work against the odds. The political circumstances have just made it a lot harder. There are a couple of theatres in Budapest that are funded by the city, not the government, so they are able to keep making work within quite harsh circumstances.

How did you get your start as a director after graduating from LAMDA?

I spent a few months doing other jobs, applying for everything and getting absolutely nothing. Then I got one of the Jerwood assistant director jobs at the Young Vic. That is what kickstarted my career. I worked on Nora: A Doll’s House with Elizabeth Freestone directing. That was a brilliant experience. After that, I did a residency supported by the European Theatre Convention at the National Theatre in Belgrade. That was great because I wanted to link myself back to that European sensibility. From there, it has been a classic process of applying for a lot of things and getting rejected a lot. I worked at the RSC on All’s Well That Ends Well with Blanche McIntyre. That was my first time working on a big Shakespeare production. It was really helpful in demystifying Shakespeare for me. Even at the RSC, they spend weeks going over the text. That helped me feel less scared.

Recently, you have worked as a staff director at the National Theatre, too. What does that involve?

The role differs depending on what venue you are in. I did Dear Octopus with Emily Burns in the Lyttleton and Coriolanus with Lyndsey Turner in the Olivier. They were essentially associate directing jobs. I worked with the understudies and ran workshops for young people. I also worked on Stephen Daldry’s production of David Lan’s The Land Of The Living in the Dorfman, where they don’t usually have understudies. That role was a lot closer to assistant directing.

It must have felt pretty cool walking into the National Theatre for work.

Honestly, it was unbelievable. I remember walking across Waterloo Bridge to the National Theatre on my first day and thinking: ‘If someone had told me I would be working in this building eight years ago, I would not have believed them.’

What did you learn there?

I learned so much. There is so much rigour and detail in all those directors’ work. The thing I really learned from Emily and Lyndsey was to do as much research as you can before you start rehearsals because that then really frees you up to respond to things in the room. I also learned how important collaboration is. When you work somewhere like the National Theatre, everyone in the building is an expert in their fields, so they will have brilliant ideas and solutions you can use.

How would you define your approach to direction?

This sounds wanky, but I think I try and bring a European sensibility to British practices. I’m interested in collaborating with writers to rework and unlock classic texts. When I started out, I was only interested in big ideas and big images, so I made lots of terrible shows. Now, I know how important for an audience to connect with a show emotionally, too. The aim of my work is always to create a piece that has a psychological truthfulness in a heightened emotional world.

Your production of Nancy Netherwood’s Radiant Boy: A Haunting at Southwark Playhouse was widely praised earlier this year. You must have been delighted?

We were thrilled. We had such a wonderful time making it. Nancy’s writing was so gorgeous. It was like a Chekhov play, but when that drama could not be contained, it exploded into horror. It had a real theatricality to it. I found that really exciting.

What does your working life involve now?

It is directing and assisting, and doing a lot of script reading and workshops. I work with NT Connections, too, which involves a lot of school visits as a mentor director. I’m still figuring out how sustainable this career is. I worked as an administrative assistant for a start-up for a long time, but I stopped doing that a year ago. At the moment, everything I am doing is related to theatre.

You are directing a new adaptation of Virginia Woolf’s The Waves by Flora Wilson Brown at Jermyn Street Theatre, too.

I am. That is surreal. Flora and I brought the idea of adapting this experimental novel to Stella Powell-Jones at Jermyn Street Theatre, and she thought it sounded great. We are so grateful for their enthusiasm and trust. There are so few small theatres that programme really ambitiously like Jermyn Street does.

Katie Mitchell did a famous multi-media adaptation of The Waves at the National Theatre in 2006.

I know. Hopefully enough time has passed that we are allowed to do it again. Actually, one of the company of The Land Of The Living, Kate Duchêne, was in Katie Mitchell’s version. She was so lovely and enthusiastic about us taking it on. She brought me in the book they used, which contained all the video images and the text from the show. Hopefully our version will feel completely new.

Petty Men is at the Arcola Theatre until December 20. A gala night, featuring a post-show panel with Professor Emma Smith, Tamara Harvey and Trevor Nunn, will take place on 26 November. The Waves is at Jermyn Street Theatre from 16 April until 23 May 2026.

Three shows to see next week

Jurassic - Soho Theatre, until November 29

You can read my interview with Foley, in which he also discusses his new radio drama Murder On Mars and his returning horror play It Walks Around The House At Night, here and get tickets for Jurassic via the button below.

p_text: Hello, and welcome to The Crush Bar, a newsletter about theatre by Fergus Morgan. This is the free Friday issue, which usually contains an interview with an exciting theatremaker or an essay on a theatre-related topic. This week, it is a chat with director Júlia Levai, whose two-man reimagining of Julius Caesar opens at the Arcola Theatre next week. After that, there are your usual three show recommendations. In case you missed it, here is this week’s issue of Shouts and Murmurs, which is a weekly round-up of the most interesting writing about theatre elsewhere… You can get Shouts and Murmurs straight in your inbox every Tuesday - and help keep this newsletter going - by signing up as a paid supporter of The Crush Bar. Theatres, companies, agencies and other organisations can also sign-up as champion supporters of The Crush Bar, which gets their name in this dedicated tab on The Crush Bar’s Substack homepage and in the footer of every Friday issue. Subscribe now As the first female director at the Royal Shakespeare Company, fired by an energetic creativity and committed Marxism, she toured bold shows around schools, staged radical productions at the company’s Stratford-upon-Avon home, and, most famously, was the driving force behind The Other Place, the RSC’s experimental third space. which opened in an old tin shed rehearsal room in 1973. Goodbody committed suicide less than two years later at the shockingly young age of 28. Now, a company, Buzz Studios, has been launched in her memory by her nephew, the actor Adam Goodbody. Its first production, a two-man reimagining of Julius Caesar at the Arcola Theatre called Petty Men, is being staged by another young director inspired by her legacy. Born and raised in Budapest, Hungary, Júlia Levai moved to Britain in 2015 to study at the University of York, then completed an MA in directing at LAMDA, graduating in 2019. Since then, she has worked as an assistant and associate director on shows at the Young Vic, the RSC, the National Theatre and elsewhere, and staged two acclaimed productions at Southwark Playhouse: Kim Davies’ Smoke, a riff on August Strindberg’s Miss Julie, in 2023 and Nancy Netherwood’s Radiant Boy: A Haunting earlier this year. Petty Men is billed as a “radical reworking” of Julius Caesar. What is it about? It is about two actors who are understudying the characters of Brutus and Cassius in an unnamed West End production of Shakespeare’s Julius Caesar. On the hundredth performance of the show, they decide to stage their own production of the play in their dressing room to tackle their boredom. As they do it, the production starts to take over and their friendship comes to a crisis point. Can they hold on to their own reality? Or do they get lost in the world of the play? You have devised the show with Adam Goodbody and John Chisham, who play Cassius and Brutus. Where did the idea come from? John and Adam brought the idea to me. At first, it was just: ‘Can we cut Julius Caesar down to two actors? Could we perform all of it through Brutus and Cassius?’ As we discussed it, quite organically, this idea of paralleling the world of theatre and the world of Rome came up, and it felt quite ripe and exciting. In the play, for example, there are two actors who are stuck in a basement while someone else gets to be the star, just as Brutus and Cassius question why Caesar has so much power in the play. And in the play, Brutus and Cassius supposedly put Rome before everything else, and there is a similar notion when it comes to theatre, too. I was interested in how far you might go to hold on to something like that. How did you go about creating it? First, we did an edit of Julius Caesar. Then, we created a structure for the show. Then, the three of us wrote a draft together, with each of us looking at different scenes. Then, we came together to develop it further. It is a piece of devised theatre, so the biggest revelations have always happened in the room. The draft we started with has drastically changed in the last week and a half. It has been a really enjoyable process of working and reworking it together as a collective. What can audiences actually expect from the show? It starts very realistically in a functioning dressing room, with a kettle, a table, a mirror, some yoga mats and all the usual bits. As it goes on, the space becomes stranger and more theatrical. It actually feels a bit closer to the kind of Shakespeare I grew up with in Hungary. In Europe, because we are working in translation, Shakespeare often becomes the starting point for creating new work. When you have to translate Shakespeare, you get much more freedom. Do you think we are too reverential with Shakespeare in the UK? I think so. When I arrived here, I was surprised by how traditional productions of Shakespeare were. I felt quite alienated by the attention to the language, and the history and tradition that surrounds that. I am more interested in how Shakespeare can be played around with. I think it is so much more thrilling to be in conversation with Shakespeare, rather than just doing it straight, which can be really alienating unless you really nail it. There are loads of brilliant productions that do that, though. I thought Emily Burns’ Measure For Measure was brilliant. She managed to find such a thriller in such a knotty play. I really wanted to see Hamlet Hail To The Thief, too. The most influential Shakespeare productions I’ve seen, though, are the ones that have come to the Barbican. I saw Thomas Ostermeier’s Richard III at the Barbican a while ago. That has really stuck with me. This is the first production from a new company, Buzz Studios. Tell me about that. It is Adam’s company. He got John and me on board for his first production. He set it up in the name of his aunt Buzz Goodbody, who, as I’m sure you know, was a pioneering female director in the 1970s. She was the first female director at the RSC and made these incredibly radical shows. She did Shakespeare in modern dress, for example. That seems obvious now, but at the time it was mind-blowing. She was passionate about making theatre accessible for young people, too. Adam has set up this company to continue her legacy. It feels really special to be part of its first production. We went to the RSC archives and went through the three boxes they have of her notes and scripts. That was really moving, especially for Adam. What was growing up in Budapest like? How did you get into theatre? I feel lucky to have grown up there when I did. When I was a teenager, there was a really vibrant theatre culture, similar to the rep system they have in Germany. There was so much good theatre going on and you could see it all essentially for free as a student. I worked as an usher at a theatre that is kind of our equivalent of the Almeida Theatre, and I saw a production of Hamlet there over and over and over again. I also had a really great teacher who was brilliant at making us feel curious and excited about theatre. She helped super shy people like me, who didn’t want to go on stage, discover that we could be directors or dramaturgs. You moved here to train, studying at the University of York, then LAMDA. Why? There were several reasons. Partly, it was because Hungary has become quite complicated politically. There is a real culture war going on against anything that encourages critical thinking, so the theatre scene has been squeezed and squeezed. I felt like if I wanted to do theatre, it was a good option to do it elsewhere. Hungary has become increasingly authoritarian under Viktor Orbán. How has that affected the country’s theatre scene? There is a lot of resistance and a lot of brilliant artists still making work against the odds. The political circumstances have just made it a lot harder. There are a couple of theatres in Budapest that are funded by the city, not the government, so they are able to keep making work within quite harsh circumstances. How did you get your start as a director after graduating from LAMDA? I spent a few months doing other jobs, applying for everything and getting absolutely nothing. Then I got one of the Jerwood assistant director jobs at the Young Vic. That is what kickstarted my career. I worked on Nora: A Doll’s House with Elizabeth Freestone directing. That was a brilliant experience. After that, I did a residency supported by the European Theatre Convention at the National Theatre in Belgrade. That was great because I wanted to link myself back to that European sensibility. From there, it has been a classic process of applying for a lot of things and getting rejected a lot. I worked at the RSC on All’s Well That Ends Well with Blanche McIntyre. That was my first time working on a big Shakespeare production. It was really helpful in demystifying Shakespeare for me. Even at the RSC, they spend weeks going over the text. That helped me feel less scared. Recently, you have worked as a staff director at the National Theatre, too. What does that involve? The role differs depending on what venue you are in. I did Dear Octopus with Emily Burns in the Lyttleton and Coriolanus with Lyndsey Turner in the Olivier. They were essentially associate directing jobs. I worked with the understudies and ran workshops for young people. I also worked on Stephen Daldry’s production of David Lan’s The Land Of The Living in the Dorfman, where they don’t usually have understudies. That role was a lot closer to assistant directing. It must have felt pretty cool walking into the National Theatre for work. Honestly, it was unbelievable. I remember walking across Waterloo Bridge to the National Theatre on my first day and thinking: ‘If someone had told me I would be working in this building eight years ago, I would not have believed them.’ What did you learn there? I learned so much. There is so much rigour and detail in all those directors’ work. The thing I really learned from Emily and Lyndsey was to do as much research as you can before you start rehearsals because that then really frees you up to respond to things in the room. I also learned how important collaboration is. When you work somewhere like the National Theatre, everyone in the building is an expert in their fields, so they will have brilliant ideas and solutions you can use. How would you define your approach to direction? This sounds wanky, but I think I try and bring a European sensibility to British practices. I’m interested in collaborating with writers to rework and unlock classic texts. When I started out, I was only interested in big ideas and big images, so I made lots of terrible shows. Now, I know how important for an audience to connect with a show emotionally, too. The aim of my work is always to create a piece that has a psychological truthfulness in a heightened emotional world. Your production of Nancy Netherwood’s Radiant Boy: A Haunting at Southwark Playhouse was widely praised earlier this year. You must have been delighted? We were thrilled. We had such a wonderful time making it. Nancy’s writing was so gorgeous. It was like a Chekhov play, but when that drama could not be contained, it exploded into horror. It had a real theatricality to it. I found that really exciting. What does your working life involve now? It is directing and assisting, and doing a lot of script reading and workshops. I work with NT Connections, too, which involves a lot of school visits as a mentor director. I’m still figuring out how sustainable this career is. I worked as an administrative assistant for a start-up for a long time, but I stopped doing that a year ago. At the moment, everything I am doing is related to theatre. You are directing a new adaptation of Virginia Woolf’s The Waves by Flora Wilson Brown at Jermyn Street Theatre, too. I am. That is surreal. Flora and I brought the idea of adapting this experimental novel to Stella Powell-Jones at Jermyn Street Theatre, and she thought it sounded great. We are so grateful for their enthusiasm and trust. There are so few small theatres that programme really ambitiously like Jermyn Street does. Katie Mitchell did a famous multi-media adaptation of The Waves at the National Theatre in 2006. I know. Hopefully enough time has passed that we are allowed to do it again. Actually, one of the company of The Land Of The Living, Kate Duchêne, was in Katie Mitchell’s version. She was so lovely and enthusiastic about us taking it on. She brought me in the book they used, which contained all the video images and the text from the show. Hopefully our version will feel completely new. Petty Men is at the Arcola Theatre until December 20. A gala night, featuring a post-show panel with Professor Emma Smith, Tamara Harvey and Trevor Nunn, will take place on 26 November. The Waves is at Jermyn Street Theatre from 16 April until 23 May 2026. Help keep The Crush Bar going by signing up as a paid supporter for £5/month or £50/year or a champion supporter for £250/year.
